2.3.5 Relation Between $P_{1\text{dB}}$ and IP3

Empirical Rule $$\text{OIP}_3 \approx OP_{1\text{dB}} + 10.64\;\text{dB}$$
Derivation of $\text{OIP}_3 \approx OP_{1\text{dB}} + 10.64$ dB (from Taylor coefficients)

Setup. From the single-tone Taylor expansion (keeping terms up to third order), the small-signal voltage gain at $\omega_0$ is

$$G_v(V_0) = a_1 + \tfrac{3}{4}\,a_3\,V_0^{\,2}$$

Step 1 — Solve for $V_{1\text{dB}}$ at the 1 dB compression point. By definition, the gain at $V_{1\text{dB}}$ has dropped by 1 dB:

$$20\log G_{1\text{dB}} \;=\; 20\log\!\left(a_1 + \tfrac{3}{4}a_3 V_{1\text{dB}}^{\,2}\right) \;=\; 20\log a_1 - 1$$

so $G_{1\text{dB}} = a_1\cdot 10^{-0.05}$. Solving the equality for $V_{1\text{dB}}$:

$$V_{1\text{dB}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4\,(10^{-0.05}-1)\,a_1}{3\,a_3}}$$

Step 2 — Compute $OP_{1\text{dB}}$. The output power at compression is $(G_{1\text{dB}}V_{1\text{dB}})^2 / 2Z_0$:

$$\begin{aligned} OP_{1\text{dB}} &= \frac{(G_{1\text{dB}}\,V_{1\text{dB}})^2}{2Z_0} = \frac{1}{2Z_0}\,(a_1\cdot 10^{-0.05})^2 \cdot \frac{4(1-10^{-0.05})\,|a_1|}{3\,|a_3|} \\[3pt] &= \frac{2\times 10^{-0.1}\,(1-10^{-0.05})}{3\,Z_0}\,\left|\frac{a_1^{\,3}}{a_3}\right| \quad\text{(W)} \end{aligned}$$

For $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ and converting to dBm:

$$\boxed{\,OP_{1\text{dB}}\,(\text{dBm}) \;=\; 10\log\!\left|\frac{a_1^{\,3}}{a_3}\right| + 0.61\,}$$

Step 3 — In the linear region, find $\text{OIP}_3$. The fundamental and IM3 output powers are

$$P_{\omega_1} = P_{\omega_2} = \frac{a_1^{\,2}\,V_0^{\,2}}{2Z_0},\qquad P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2} = P_{2\omega_2-\omega_1} = \frac{1}{2Z_0}\left(\tfrac{3}{4}a_3\,V_0^{\,3}\right)^2 = \frac{9\,a_3^{\,2}\,V_0^{\,6}}{32\,Z_0}$$

At the intercept $V_{\text{IP}_3}$, the two are equal: $P_{\omega_1}(V_{\text{IP}_3}) = P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}(V_{\text{IP}_3})$. Solving:

$$V_{\text{IP}_3} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4\,a_1}{3\,|a_3|}}$$

Substituting back gives the output intercept power:

$$\text{OIP}_3 \;=\; \frac{a_1^{\,2}\,V_{\text{IP}_3}^{\,2}}{2Z_0} \;=\; \frac{2\,a_1^{\,3}}{3\,Z_0\,|a_3|}\quad\text{(W)}$$

For $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ in dBm:

$$\boxed{\,\text{OIP}_3\,(\text{dBm}) \;=\; 10\log\!\left|\frac{a_1^{\,3}}{a_3}\right| + 11.25\,}$$

Step 4 — Subtract. Both expressions share the same $10\log|a_1^{\,3}/a_3|$ term, so

$$\boxed{\,\text{OIP}_3\,(\text{dBm}) - OP_{1\text{dB}}\,(\text{dBm}) \;=\; 11.25 - 0.61 \;=\; 10.64\ \text{dB}\,}$$

Bonus — the 3:1 slope rule for IM3. Take the ratio

$$\frac{P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}}{(P_{\omega_1})^3} \;=\; \frac{9\,Z_0^{\,2}\,a_3^{\,2}}{4\,a_1^{\,6}} \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3}\right)^{\!2}$$

which gives the textbook IM3-from-fundamental formula in dB:

$$\boxed{\,P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}\,(\text{dBm}) \;=\; 3\,P_{\omega_1}\,(\text{dBm}) - 2\,\text{OIP}_3\,(\text{dBm})\,}$$

Every 1 dB rise in $P_{\omega_1}$ adds 3 dB to the IM3 product, so the gap shrinks by 2 dB per dB of drive — that is the dynamic-range squeeze you fight with cascaded IIP3 budgeting.

Lecture Example — Estimating OP1dB and OPsf

$G=10$ dB, $F=5$ dB, $\text{OIP}_3 = 20$ dBm, $T_0=290$ K, $B=1$ MHz.

$OP_{1\text{dB}} = 20 - 10.64 = 9.36$ dBm

$N_o = -99$ dBm. $OP_{sf} = \frac{2}{3}(9.36) + 7.09 + \frac{1}{3}(-99) = -19.67$ dBm

Practice Example 19 — P1dB from IP3

Problem: A mixer has $\text{IIP}_3 = +5$ dBm and conversion loss $L = 7$ dB. Estimate $IP_{1\text{dB}}$ and $OP_{1\text{dB}}$.

Click to reveal solution

$\text{OIP}_3 = \text{IIP}_3 + G = 5 + (-7) = -2$ dBm

$OP_{1\text{dB}} = \text{OIP}_3 - 10.64 = -2 - 10.64 = -12.64$ dBm

$IP_{1\text{dB}} = OP_{1\text{dB}} - G = -12.64 - (-7) = -5.64$ dBm

(Rule of thumb check: $\text{IIP}_3 - IP_{1\text{dB}} \approx 5 - (-5.6) = 10.6$ dB ✓)

2.3.6 Dynamic Range

Linear dynamic range ($DR_l$)

➤A power range limited at the low end by noise and at the high end by the compression point. This is usually considered the usable power range for a linear power amplifier.

$$DR_l \equiv \frac{OP_{1\text{dB}}}{N_o} = \frac{OP_{1\text{dB}}}{kT_0\,B\cdot G_A\cdot F}$$

Spurious-free dynamic range ($DR_f$)

➤A power range limited at the low end by the third-order intermodulation product equal to the noise level and at the high end by the corresponding output signal power. This is usually considered the usable power range for low-noise amplifiers or mixers in a receiver.

$$DR_f \equiv \left.\frac{P_{\omega_1}}{P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}}\right|_{\,P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}=N_o}$$

$P_{\text{out}}$ vs $P_{\text{in}}$ — the two dynamic ranges $DR_l$ (linear) and $DR_f$ (spurious-free) sit between the noise floor $N_o$ and the compression / IM3 corners:

Derivation — $DR_f$, $DR_l$, and $OP_{sf}$ in dB form

Setup. From the 3:1 IM3 slope rule (§3.5), the fundamental output power can be written in terms of IM3 and OIP3:

$$P_{\omega_1} \;=\; \bigl(P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}\bigr)^{\!1/3}\,(\text{OIP}_3)^{2/3}$$

Step 1 — $DR_f$ in linear form. Substitute into the $DR_f$ definition with the constraint $P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}=N_o$:

$$DR_f \;=\; \left.\frac{P_{\omega_1}}{P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}}\right|_{P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}=N_o} \;=\; \left.\frac{(P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2})^{1/3}(\text{OIP}_3)^{2/3}}{P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}}\right|_{P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}=N_o} \;=\; \left(\frac{\text{OIP}_3}{N_o}\right)^{\!2/3} \;=\; \left(\frac{\text{OIP}_3}{kT_0\,B\cdot G_A\cdot F}\right)^{\!2/3}$$

In dB:

$$\boxed{\,DR_f\,(\text{dB}) \;=\; \tfrac{2}{3}\,\text{OIP}_3\,(\text{dBm}) - \tfrac{2}{3}\,N_o\,(\text{dBm})\,}$$

Step 2 — $DR_l$ in dB form. Directly from the definition $DR_l = OP_{1\text{dB}}/N_o$:

$$\boxed{\,DR_l\,(\text{dB}) \;=\; OP_{1\text{dB}}\,(\text{dBm}) - N_o\,(\text{dBm})\,}$$

Step 3 — relate $DR_l$ and $DR_f$. Solving Step 1 for $\text{OIP}_3$ and substituting into Step 2:

$$DR_l\,(\text{dB}) \;=\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,DR_f\,(\text{dB}) + OP_{1\text{dB}}\,(\text{dBm}) - \text{OIP}_3\,(\text{dBm})$$

Using §3.5's empirical rule $\text{OIP}_3 - OP_{1\text{dB}} = 10.64$ dB:

$$\boxed{\,DR_l\,(\text{dB}) \;=\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,DR_f\,(\text{dB}) - 10.64\ \text{dB}\,}$$

Comparing the two ranges:

  • $DR_f > 21.28$ dB  ⇒  $DR_l > DR_f$
  • $DR_f = 21.28$ dB  ⇒  $DR_l = DR_f$ (crossover)
  • $DR_f < 21.28$ dB  ⇒  $DR_l < DR_f$

Step 4 — output spurious-free power $OP_{sf}$. The fundamental output level at which the IM3 product equals the noise floor:

$$\begin{aligned} OP_{sf}\,(\text{dBm}) &\equiv \left.P_{\omega_1}\right|_{P_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}=N_o} \;=\; DR_f\,(\text{dB}) + N_o\,(\text{dBm}) \\[3pt] &=\; \tfrac{2}{3}\,\text{OIP}_3\,(\text{dBm}) + \tfrac{1}{3}\,N_o\,(\text{dBm}) \\[3pt] &=\; \tfrac{2}{3}\,OP_{1\text{dB}}\,(\text{dBm}) + 7.09\,(\text{dBm}) + \tfrac{1}{3}\,N_o\,(\text{dBm}) \end{aligned}$$

The constant $7.09 = \tfrac{2}{3}\times 10.64$ dB carries the OIP3-vs-P1dB offset into the spur-free output expression.

With minimum detectable SNR specified: $DR_f = \tfrac{2}{3}[\text{OIP}_3 - N_o] - \text{SNR}_{min}$.

Always: $DR_l - DR_f \approx 21.3$ dB.

Lecture Example — Dynamic Ranges

$F=7$ dB, $OP_{1\text{dB}}=25$ dBm, $G=40$ dB, $\text{OIP}_3=35$ dBm, $T_a=150$ K, SNR$_{min}$=10 dB, $B=100$ MHz.

$N_o = GkB[T_a+(F-1)T_0] = 10^4\times1.38\times10^{-23}\times10^8\times[150+4.01\times290] = -47.4$ dBm

$DR_l = 25 - (-47.4) = 72.4$ dB

$DR_f = \frac{2}{3}[35-(-47.4)] - 10 = \frac{2}{3}(82.4) - 10 = 54.9 - 10 = 44.9$ dB

Practice Example 20 — Spurious-Free Dynamic Range

Problem: A receiver has $\text{OIP}_3 = 25$ dBm, $G = 30$ dB, $F = 4$ dB, $B = 10$ MHz, $T_0 = 290$ K. Find $DR_l$ and $DR_f$ (no minimum SNR constraint).

Click to reveal solution

$N_o = kT_0B\cdot G\cdot F$ (since source is at $T_0$)

$= 1.38\times10^{-23}\times290\times10^7\times10^3\times10^{0.4}$

$= 4\times10^{-14}\times1000\times2.512 = 1.005\times10^{-10}$ W $= -70.0$ dBm

$OP_{1\text{dB}} = \text{OIP}_3 - 10.64 = 25 - 10.64 = 14.36$ dBm

$DR_l = 14.36 - (-70.0) = 84.4$ dB

$DR_f = \frac{2}{3}[25 - (-70.0)] = \frac{2}{3}\times95 = 63.3$ dB

Check: $DR_l - DR_f = 84.4 - 63.3 = 21.1$ dB $\approx 21.3$ dB ✓

2.3.7 Intercept Point of Cascaded Components

Two-stage cascade and its equivalent single-block representation:

At the output of the first stage:

$$P'_{2\omega_1-\omega_2} \;=\; \frac{(P'_{\omega_1})^3}{(\text{OIP}_3')^2}\;\Longrightarrow\;V'_{2\omega_1-\omega_2} \;=\; \sqrt{2 Z_0\cdot P'_{2\omega_1-\omega_2}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2 Z_0\,(P'_{\omega_1})^3}}{\text{OIP}_3'}$$

At the output of the second stage:

$$V''_{2\omega_1-\omega_2} \;=\; \sqrt{G_2}\cdot V'_{2\omega_1-\omega_2} \;+\; \frac{\sqrt{2 Z_0\,(P''_{\omega_1})^3}}{\text{OIP}_3''} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2 G_2 Z_0\,(P'_{\omega_1})^3}}{\text{OIP}_3'} \;+\; \frac{\sqrt{2 Z_0\,(P''_{\omega_1})^3}}{\text{OIP}_3''}$$

Since $P''_{\omega_1} = G_2\,P'_{\omega_1}$:

$$V''_{2\omega_1-\omega_2} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2 G_2 Z_0\,(P''_{\omega_1}/G_2)^3}}{\text{OIP}_3'} \;+\; \frac{\sqrt{2 Z_0\,(P''_{\omega_1})^3}}{\text{OIP}_3''} \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{G_2\cdot\text{OIP}_3'} + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3''}\right)\sqrt{2 Z_0\,(P''_{\omega_1})^3}$$ $$P''_{2\omega_1-\omega_2} \;=\; \frac{(V''_{2\omega_1-\omega_2})^2}{2Z_0} \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{G_2\cdot\text{OIP}_3'} + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3''}\right)^{\!2}\!(P''_{\omega_1})^3 \;\equiv\; \frac{(P''_{\omega_1})^3}{(\text{OIP}_3)^2}$$

where $\text{OIP}_3$ is the equivalent output third-order intercept point of the cascaded network.

$$\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3} \;=\; \frac{1}{G_2\cdot\text{OIP}_3'} \;+\; \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3''}$$

In general, for $N$ networks that are cascaded:

$$\frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3} \;=\; \frac{1}{G_2 G_3\cdots G_N\cdot\text{OIP}_3^{(1)}} \;+\; \frac{1}{G_3\cdots G_N\cdot\text{OIP}_3^{(2)}} \;+\; \cdots \;+\; \frac{1}{G_N\cdot\text{OIP}_3^{(N-1)}} \;+\; \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(N)}}$$

With overall gain $G = G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}\,G_N$:

$$\frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3} \;=\; \frac{1}{G}\!\left(\frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(1)}/G_1} + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(2)}/(G_1 G_2)} + \cdots + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(N-1)}/(G_1 G_2\cdots G_{N-1})} + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(N)}/(G_1 G_2\cdots G_{N-1}\,G_N)}\right)$$

The above expression shows that the third-order intercept point of a cascaded system is dominated by the last few stages, since the later stages divided by a larger product of the gains reduce $\text{IP}_3$ more significantly than the earlier stages.

Cascaded OIP3 — output-referred $$\frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3} = \frac{1}{G_2 G_3\cdots G_N\cdot\text{OIP}_3^{(1)}} + \frac{1}{G_3\cdots G_N\cdot\text{OIP}_3^{(2)}} + \cdots + \frac{1}{G_N\cdot\text{OIP}_3^{(N-1)}} + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(N)}}$$
Contrast with Noise Figure

Noise figure: first stage dominates (later stages divided by preceding gain).

IP3: last stage dominates (later stages multiplied by preceding gain).

This creates a fundamental design tension in receiver chains.

Lecture Example — Cascaded IP3 (Amplifier + Mixer)

Amplifier: $G=20$ dB, $\text{OIP}'_3=22$ dBm $=158$ mW. Mixer: $L=6$ dB ($G_2=0.25$), $\text{IIP}''_3=13$ dBm → $\text{OIP}''_3 = 7$ dBm $=5$ mW.

$\frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3} = \frac{1}{0.25\times158} + \frac{1}{5} = \frac{1}{39.5} + \frac{1}{5} = 0.0253 + 0.200 = 0.2253$

$\text{OIP}_3 = 4.4$ mW $= 6.4$ dBm. (Dominated by the mixer's OIP3!)

Practice Example 21 — Three-Stage Cascaded IP3

Problem: Three cascaded stages: LNA ($G_1=15$ dB, $\text{OIP}_3^{(1)}=25$ dBm), Filter ($G_2=-2$ dB, $\text{OIP}_3^{(2)}=40$ dBm), Mixer ($G_3=-8$ dB, $\text{OIP}_3^{(3)}=10$ dBm). Find the overall OIP3.

Click to reveal solution

Convert: $G_1=31.62$, $G_2=0.631$, $G_3=0.158$. $G_{total} = 31.62\times0.631\times0.158 = 3.15 = 4.98$ dB.

$\text{OIP}_3^{(1)}=316.2$ mW, $\text{OIP}_3^{(2)}=10{,}000$ mW, $\text{OIP}_3^{(3)}=10$ mW.

Using the formula (all powers in mW, referred to overall output):

$\frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3} = \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(1)}\cdot G_2 G_3} + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(2)}\cdot G_3} + \frac{1}{\text{OIP}_3^{(3)}}$

$= \frac{1}{316.2\times0.631\times0.158} + \frac{1}{10000\times0.158} + \frac{1}{10}$

$= \frac{1}{31.52} + \frac{1}{1580} + \frac{1}{10} = 0.0317 + 0.000633 + 0.100 = 0.1324$

$\text{OIP}_3 = 7.55$ mW $= 8.78$ dBm

(Dominated by the mixer — the last-stage term $1/10 = 0.100$ is the largest contributor.)

2.3.8 Passive Intermodulation (PIM)

➤Intermodulation products can also be generated by passive nonlinear effects — anywhere there is a metal-to-metal contact (connectors, cables, antennas, etc.). The result is called passive intermodulation (PIM).

Common causes of PIM

  • Poor mechanical contact at RF junctions
  • Oxidation between ferrous-based metals
  • Contamination of conducting surfaces at RF junctions
  • Use of nonlinear materials such as carbon-fiber composites or ferromagnetic metals
  • Thermal effects when high powers heat junctions (adds to overall nonlinearity)

When PIM matters

  • Third-order coefficients of passive components are normally very small — PIM is usually only significant when input powers are large.
  • Cellular base-station transmitters are the canonical case: per-channel powers of 30–40 dBm with many closely spaced RF channels.
  • Typical PIM target: keep PIM below −125 dBm with two simultaneous 40-dBm transmit signals — a dynamic range of $\sim$165 dB.
  • Receivers usually do not have a PIM problem because power levels are far lower.

Design and maintenance implications

  • Predicting PIM from first principles is very difficult — measurement is the standard approach.
  • Carefully select cables, connectors, and antenna components used in the high-power portions of the transmitter.
  • Outdoor exposure (oxidation, vibration, sunlight) degrades passive components over time, so a regular maintenance program is essential.

Problems & Exercises

A problem bank covering every topic in this chapter — noise sources & thermal noise, PSD & autocorrelation, modulation/BER, noise figure & temperature, Friis cascade, antenna noise, link budgets, and nonlinear distortion (P1dB, IP3, dynamic range). Click ? to flag a problem for review or ✓ to mark it done — state persists in your browser.

Part 1 — Noise & BER Part 2 — Noise Figure & Temperature Part 3 — Distortion (IP3)
Part 1 — Noise & BER in Linear Receivers 22 problems
2.1.1 Easy Tier 1 §1.1 noise sources ? ✓
List the five noise types covered in §1.1 (thermal, shot, flicker, plasma, quantum). For each, name its physical origin and identify which one dominates inside a discrete-component microwave receiver at room temperature.
2.1.2 Easy Tier 1 §1.2 thermal noise ? ✓
A 50 $\Omega$ resistor at $T = 290$ K feeds a matched load through a 10 MHz bandpass filter. Compute (a) the rms open-circuit noise voltage $v_n^s$, (b) the available noise power $P_n = kTB$ in W and in dBm.
2.1.3 Easy Tier 1 §1.2 kT₀ floor ? ✓
Show that $kT_0 = -174$ dBm/Hz at $T_0 = 290$ K. Then write the noise floor in dBm for $B = 1$ kHz, 1 MHz, and 1 GHz. Why is $-174$ dBm/Hz called the "thermal noise floor"?
2.1.4 Medium Tier 1 §1.3 PSD ↔ power ? ✓
White noise has two-sided PSD $S(f) = n_0/2$. Show that an ideal bandpass filter of bandwidth $B$ centred at $\pm f_c$ delivers an output noise power $P_n = n_0 B = kTB$. Sketch the integration region on the PSD plot.
2.1.5 Medium Tier 1 §1.3 autocorrelation ? ✓
Use the Wiener–Khinchin theorem to derive $R(\tau) = (n_0/2)\,\delta(\tau)$ from $S(f) = n_0/2$. Explain what this implies about the correlation between $n(t)$ and $n(t+\tau)$ for any $\tau \neq 0$, and why white noise has infinite total power.
2.1.6 Medium Tier 2 §1.3b Parseval ? ✓
State Parseval's theorem and use it to show that $R(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(f)\,df$ equals the time-average power of the random process. Why is $R(0)$ the natural definition of "total power"?
2.1.7 Medium Tier 1 §1.4 SSB / DSB-SC SNR ? ✓
A coherent demodulator multiplies the received signal by $2\cos\omega_c t$ and lowpass-filters. Show that for SSB the output SNR equals the input SNR, while for DSB-SC the output SNR is 3 dB higher than the input SNR. What physical mechanism gives DSB-SC its 3 dB advantage?
2.1.8 Easy Tier 1 §1.5 BER curve ? ✓
For coherent BPSK, $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/n_0}\right)$. Find $E_b/n_0$ (in dB) needed for $P_e = 10^{-3}$, $10^{-5}$, and $10^{-9}$. Discuss the steepness of the BER curve in the operating range.
2.1.9 Hard Tier 2 §1.6 integrate-and-dump ? ✓
A bit waveform $\pm V\cos\omega_c t$ of duration $T$ is multiplied by $\cos\omega_c t$ and integrated over one bit. Derive (a) the signal output amplitude $VT/2$ (or $VT$ depending on the chosen convention), (b) the noise variance $\sigma_{out}^2 = n_0/T$, and (c) show the matched-filter SNR equals $2E_b/n_0$.
2.1.10 Easy Tier 2 §1.7 AM efficiency ? ✓
For DSB-LC AM the efficiency is $\eta = m^2/(2 + m^2)$, $0 \le m \le 1$. Compute $\eta$ for $m = 0.3$, $0.5$, $0.7$, $1.0$. Explain why DSB-LC wastes ≥ 67 % of transmitted power even at full modulation.
2.1.11 Medium Tier 1 §1.7 modulation table ? ✓
For BER $= 10^{-5}$, list the required $E_b/n_0$ (dB) for coherent BPSK, coherent FSK, coherent ASK, non-coherent FSK, and non-coherent ASK. Which is most power-efficient and which is least? By how many dB?
2.1.12 Medium Tier 1 §1.7 spectral efficiency ? ✓
Compute the bandwidth efficiency $\eta_s = R_b / B$ (bps/Hz) for BPSK, QPSK, 8-PSK, 16-QAM, and 64-QAM. Then, for $R_b = 24$ Mbps and $B = 6$ MHz, identify the lowest-order modulation that fits.
2.1.13 Hard Tier 1 §1.7 QPSK link min SNR ? ✓
An IS-54 QPSK link runs $R_b = 48.6$ kbps in $B = 30$ kHz. Find the minimum SNR (dB) at the demodulator input for BER $= 10^{-5}$ using $S/N = (E_b/n_0)(R_b/B)$ and $E_b/n_0|_{10^{-5}} = 9.6$ dB.
2.1.14 Hard Tier 2 §1.8 16-QAM ? ✓
A 16-QAM system needs BER $= 10^{-5}$ ($E_b/n_0 \approx 14.4$ dB) at $R_b = 12$ Mbps in $B = 4$ MHz. (a) Find $S/N$ at the demod input (dB). (b) Compare to QPSK at the same $R_b$ — by how many dB does 16-QAM cost more SNR for half the bandwidth?
2.1.15 Easy Tier 1 📖 From textbook §1.1 noise sources ? ✓
Identify the dominant internal noise mechanism in each device at room temperature: (a) GaAs HEMT LNA at 4 GHz, (b) Si BJT mixer at 100 MHz operating at low collector current, (c) varactor frequency multiplier at 8 GHz. Briefly justify each choice in one sentence.
2.1.16 Easy Tier 1 📖 From textbook §1.2 thermal noise ? ✓
A 2.2 k$\Omega$ resistor at $T=300$ K drives a unity-gain buffer over a 5 MHz noise bandwidth. (a) Compute the rms open-circuit thermal-noise voltage $v_n^{rms}$ at the buffer input. (b) Convert to available noise power in dBm. (c) How does $v_n^{rms}$ change if you cool the resistor to 77 K?
2.1.17 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §1.3 PSD ↔ power ? ✓
A wideband noise source has constant two-sided PSD $S_n(f) = 2\times 10^{-18}$ V$^2$/Hz across 0 – 1 GHz. The source feeds a first-order RC low-pass filter ($f_c = 100$ MHz) into a 50 $\Omega$ load. Find the rms noise voltage at the filter output and the equivalent noise bandwidth $B_n$ of the filter.
2.1.18 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §1.4 SSB / DSB-SC SNR ? ✓
An SSB receiver detects a coherent tone of amplitude $A_c = 1$ mV (rms) buried in white noise with PSD $n_0 = -174$ dBm/Hz across a 30 kHz audio passband. (a) Find input SNR. (b) Find output SNR after the synchronous demodulator. (c) State the SNR improvement in dB and justify it from the demodulator gain.
2.1.19 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §1.5 BER ? ✓
For coherent BPSK at $E_b/n_0 = 7.0$ dB, compute the bit error probability using $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2 E_b/n_0})$. Then estimate the BER for 64-QAM at the same $E_b/n_0$. Comment on which modulation gives lower BER and why this is consistent with the 'spectral-efficiency vs. SNR' trade.
2.1.20 Medium Tier 2 📖 From textbook §1.6 integrate-and-dump ? ✓
A binary baseband signal with bit-rate $R_b = 1$ Mbps is detected with (a) a matched filter (integrate-and-dump) and (b) a brick-wall lowpass at $B = 1.5$ MHz. Both at $E_b/n_0 = 9$ dB. Compare the resulting BERs and explain in one sentence why the matched filter wins.
2.1.21 Hard Tier 1 📖 From textbook §1.7 modulation comparison ? ✓
Construct a comparison table for BPSK, 8-PSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM and 256-QAM at BER $= 10^{-6}$ over an AWGN channel: required $E_b/n_0$ (dB), spectral efficiency $R_b/B$ (bps/Hz, theoretical max), and the dB SNR penalty relative to BPSK. Briefly note where each scheme is used in modern systems (Wi-Fi, LTE, 5G NR, DVB-S2).
2.1.22 Hard Tier 2 📖 From textbook §1.8 16-QAM ? ✓
A 16-QAM downlink transmits $R_b = 80$ Mbps in $B = 25$ MHz to achieve BER $= 10^{-5}$. (a) Find symbol rate, bits/symbol, and spectral efficiency. (b) Required $E_b/n_0$ from a textbook curve. (c) Required $S/N$ at the demod input in dB. (d) If the system noise temperature $T_{sys} = 400$ K, what minimum received signal power $P_r$ (dBm) is needed?
Part 2 — Noise Temperature, Noise Figure, Antenna & Link Budget 25 problems
2.2.1 Easy Tier 1 §2.1 F ↔ Tₑ ? ✓
Convert the following noise figures to equivalent noise temperatures (assume $T_0 = 290$ K): $F = 1$ dB, 3 dB, 6 dB, 10 dB. Then go the other way: convert $T_e = 50$ K, 170 K, 580 K, 2610 K to $F$ in dB.
2.2.2 Medium Tier 1 §2.1 amplifier Tₑ from gain ? ✓
An LNA has $G = 20$ dB and adds $4\times10^{-13}$ W of output noise in $B = 100$ MHz at the matched load. Compute the equivalent input noise temperature $T_e$ and the noise figure $F$ (dB).
2.2.3 Medium Tier 1 §2.2 Y-factor ? ✓
A Y-factor measurement uses a hot load at $T_h = 873$ K and a cold load at $T_c = 77$ K. The measured noise-power ratio is $Y = N_h / N_c = 2.50$. Compute (a) $T_e$ of the device under test, (b) noise figure $F$ in dB.
2.2.4 Easy Tier 1 §2.4 lossy line ? ✓
A coaxial cable has insertion loss 2.5 dB at $T_p = T_0 = 290$ K. State the noise figure $F$ in dB and compute the equivalent noise temperature $T_e$. Why does $F = L$ exactly when the line is at $T_0$?
2.2.5 Medium Tier 2 §2.4 cold lossy line ? ✓
A waveguide feed line ($L = 3$ dB) is mounted on a satellite at physical temperature $T_p = 200$ K. Compute $T_e$ and contrast with the value you'd get if you naively assumed $T_p = 290$ K. Why does cooling a passive lossy line reduce its noise contribution?
2.2.6 Medium Tier 1 §2.5 Friis cascade ? ✓
A 3-stage receiver: LNA $F_1 = 2$ dB, $G_1 = 15$ dB; mixer $F_2 = 7$ dB, $G_2 = -6$ dB; IF amplifier $F_3 = 4$ dB, $G_3 = 30$ dB. Compute the cascaded noise figure $F$ (dB) and the equivalent input noise temperature $T_e$.
2.2.7 Medium Tier 1 §2.5 loss-before-LNA penalty ? ✓
Compare two architectures with the same LNA ($F = 2$ dB, $G = 20$ dB) followed by a mixer ($F = 8$ dB, $G = -6$ dB) and IF amp ($F = 4$ dB, $G = 30$ dB):
(a) clean: antenna → LNA → mixer → IF;
(b) with cable: antenna → 1.5 dB cable → LNA → mixer → IF.
By how many dB does the cable degrade the system NF? State the design rule it illustrates.
2.2.8 Medium Tier 2 §2.6 Wilkinson ? ✓
A matched, lossless Wilkinson power divider has 3 dB insertion loss between input and each output port. Justify why $F = 3$ dB at each output port even though the divider is dissipationless. Why is this not a contradiction?
2.2.9 Easy Tier 1 §2.7 antenna noise ? ✓
Compute the antenna noise temperature $T_a$ for a satellite earth-station antenna with radiation efficiency $\eta_{rad} = 0.9$, brightness temperature $T_b = 50$ K (sky), and physical temperature $T_p = 290$ K. How does $T_a$ change if the antenna is rotated to look at the ground ($T_b = 290$ K)?
2.2.10 Easy Tier 1 §2.7 EIRP & FSPL ? ✓
A DBS satellite at $R = 36{,}000$ km transmits at $f = 12$ GHz with $P_t = 100$ W and $G_t = 35$ dBi. Compute (a) EIRP in dBW, (b) free-space path loss in dB, (c) received power $P_r$ at a $G_r = 30$ dBi dish.
2.2.11 Medium Tier 1 §2.8 G/T ? ✓
A receive antenna has $G_r = 30$ dBi, $T_a = 80$ K, and feeds a receiver with $T_e = 120$ K (no feed line). Compute the figure of merit $G/T$ in dB/K. If the LNA is upgraded so $T_e \to 60$ K, by how many dB does $G/T$ improve?
2.2.12 Hard Tier 1 §2.8 link budget CNR ? ✓
For the DBS link of Problem 2.10, with $G/T = 12$ dB/K and $B = 27$ MHz, compute CNR using
$\text{CNR} = \text{EIRP} + G/T - \text{FSPL} - 10\log(kB)$.
Recall $10\log(kB) = -228.6 + 10\log B$. Is the CNR sufficient for QPSK at BER $= 10^{-6}$?
2.2.13 Hard Tier 2 §2.9 minimum detectable ? ✓
A receiver has $F = 3$ dB, $B = 1$ MHz, antenna $T_a = 100$ K, and the demodulator requires $(S_o/N_o)_{min} = 10$ dB. Compute the minimum detectable signal $S_{i,min}$ using $S_{i,min} = kB[\,T_a + (F-1)T_0\,](S_o/N_o)_{min}$. Why is the common formula $S_{i,min} = kT_0BF\cdot(S_o/N_o)_{min}$ wrong here?
2.2.14 Hard Tier 2 §2.9 full receiver Tₛᵧₛ ? ✓
An antenna ($T_a = 60$ K) feeds a transmission line with $L_T = 1.5$ dB at $T_p = 290$ K, then a receiver with $F = 2$ dB. Compute (a) $T_{TL}$, (b) the system noise temperature referred to the antenna $T_{SYS} = T_a + T_{TL} + L_T T_{REC}$, (c) the equivalent system noise figure.
2.2.15 Hard Tier 3 HW Pozar 14.6 ? ✓
(Pozar 14.6) Repeat the full receiver analysis for $T_a = 200$ K, transmission line $L_T = 2$ dB at $T_p = 270$ K, receiver $F = 4$ dB. Then redo it assuming the antenna sits inside a heated shelter at $T_a = T_0 = 290$ K and verify that $T_{SYS}$ collapses to $T_0 \cdot F_{tot}$.
2.2.16 Hard Tier 3 HW H3 design ? ✓
Design exercise: budget a 64-QAM downlink at $R_b = 100$ Mbps, BER $= 10^{-6}$ ($E_b/n_0 \approx 22.5$ dB). Pick reasonable values for $B$, $G_t$, $P_t$, $R$, $G_r$, $T_a$, $T_e$, and verify the link closes with a $\ge 3$ dB margin. Identify the bottleneck.
2.2.17 Easy Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.1 equivalent Tₑ ? ✓
An amplifier has noise figure $F = 2.5$ dB measured at $T_0 = 290$ K. (a) Convert to equivalent noise temperature $T_e$. (b) The amplifier is now driven by a source at $T_s = 100$ K (cooled). Compute the new system noise temperature $T_{sys} = T_s + T_e$ and the corresponding effective $F$ (at this new source temperature).
2.2.18 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.2 Y-factor ? ✓
A Y-factor measurement on an LNA gives $Y = 1.45$ ($\approx 1.61$ dB) using calibrated noise loads $T_h = 470$ K (hot) and $T_c = 80$ K (cold). Compute $T_e$ and $F$ (in dB) of the device under test.
2.2.19 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.3 noise figure ? ✓
A receiver chain has $G_{tot} = 30$ dB, $F = 4$ dB, $B = 10$ MHz, $T_0 = 290$ K. (a) Compute the input-referred noise power $kT_0BF$ (dBm). (b) Compute the output noise power $G\cdot kT_0BF$. (c) If the input signal is $S_i = -80$ dBm, find SNR$_i$ and SNR$_o$.
2.2.20 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.4 lossy line ? ✓
A 50 $\Omega$ feedline with insertion loss $L = 2$ dB at $T_p = 290$ K precedes an LNA whose noise figure is $F_{LNA} = 1.5$ dB. (a) Compute $F_{line}$ and $T_{line}$. (b) Compute the combined two-stage noise figure $F_{cascade}$. (c) How many dB does the line cost you in receiver sensitivity?
2.2.21 Hard Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.5 Friis cascade ? ✓
Three-stage receiver, all at $T_0 = 290$ K: LNA ($G_1 = 15$ dB, $F_1 = 2$ dB), Mixer ($L_2 = 6$ dB, $F_2 = 8$ dB), IF Amp ($G_3 = 30$ dB, $F_3 = 2$ dB). Compute $F_{tot}$ (dB) using the Friis cascade formula. Identify which stage dominates noise figure and why.
2.2.22 Medium Tier 2 📖 From textbook §2.6 mismatch / Wilkinson ? ✓
A two-way 0° Wilkinson divider has the ideal 3 dB power split plus an extra 0.3 dB insertion loss in each arm. All ports at $T_p = 290$ K. (a) Compute the noise temperature $T_e$ added by the divider, looking from one output port. (b) State the noise figure of one divider arm in dB.
2.2.23 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.7 antenna Tₐ & Friis tx ? ✓
A 12 GHz earth-station antenna with radiation efficiency $\eta_{rad} = 0.85$ points at cold sky $T_{sky} = 20$ K. Antenna physical temperature $T_p = 290$ K. (a) Find $T_a$. (b) The receiver behind the antenna has $T_e = 60$ K. Find $T_{sys}$. (c) Compute the noise floor in a 36 MHz transponder bandwidth.
2.2.24 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.8 G/T figure of merit ? ✓
An earth station has receive-antenna gain $G_r = 45$ dBi and total system noise temperature $T_{sys} = 120$ K. (a) Compute $G/T$ in dB/K. (b) The downlink delivers an EIRP of 50 dBW with free-space loss FSPL $= 205$ dB at the slant range. Compute $C/N_0$ (dB-Hz) using only EIRP, FSPL, $G/T$, and Boltzmann's constant.
2.2.25 Hard Tier 1 📖 From textbook §2.9 full receiver analysis ? ✓
A 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi receiver: $T_a = 200$ K, transmission line $L_T = 0.5$ dB at $T_p = 290$ K, receiver $T_{REC} = 180$ K, $B = 20$ MHz. The received signal at the antenna port is $S_i = -80$ dBm. Find $T_{TL}$, $T_{SYS}$, the input noise power, and the output SNR.
Part 3 — Gain Compression & Intermodulation Distortion 20 problems
2.3.1 Easy Tier 1 §3.1 Taylor model ? ✓
A weakly nonlinear amplifier is modeled $v_o = a_1 v_i + a_2 v_i^2 + a_3 v_i^3$. For a single-tone input $v_i = A\cos\omega t$, identify which Taylor coefficient produces (a) the small-signal gain, (b) DC offset and the 2nd harmonic, (c) gain compression / expansion, (d) the 3rd harmonic. State the sign of $a_3$ for compression.
2.3.2 Easy Tier 1 §3.2 P1dB ? ✓
An amplifier has small-signal gain $G = 20$ dB and input 1-dB compression point IP1dB $= -10$ dBm. Compute OP1dB. What is the relation $\text{OP1dB} = \text{IP1dB} + G - 1$ telling you?
2.3.3 Medium Tier 1 §3.3 IM products ? ✓
Two tones at $f_1 = 1.000$ GHz and $f_2 = 1.001$ GHz drive a nonlinear amp. List all 2nd- and 3rd-order intermodulation products and identify which fall in-band (close to $f_1, f_2$) and which fall far out-of-band. Why is IM3 the design-limiting product?
2.3.4 Medium Tier 1 §3.4 IP3 from two-tone ? ✓
A two-tone test produces a fundamental output of $-10$ dBm/tone and an IM3 output of $-70$ dBm. Compute OIP3. State the 1:1 / 3:1 slope rule and explain why OIP3 is the extrapolated intersection.
2.3.5 Medium Tier 1 §3.4 IM3 level ? ✓
An amplifier has $G = 20$ dB and OIP3 $= 30$ dBm. Two tones at $-30$ dBm/tone are applied. Compute (a) the fundamental output level/tone, (b) the IM3 output level using $P_{IM3} = 3 P_{out} - 2 \cdot \text{OIP3}$.
2.3.6 Medium Tier 1 §3.5 IP3 ↔ P1dB ? ✓
Use the rule of thumb $\text{OIP3} \approx \text{OP1dB} + 10.6$ dB. (a) For OP1dB $= 22$ dBm, estimate OIP3. (b) Conversely, for a measured OIP3 $= 35$ dBm, estimate OP1dB. (c) When does this rule break down (technology, biasing, output structure)?
2.3.7 Medium Tier 2 §3.6 dynamic range ? ✓
An amplifier has output noise floor $N_o = -90$ dBm, OP1dB $= 20$ dBm, OIP3 $= 30$ dBm. Compute (a) the linear dynamic range $DR_l = \text{OP1dB} - N_o$, (b) the spurious-free dynamic range $DR_f = \tfrac{2}{3}(\text{OIP3} - N_o)$, (c) verify $DR_l - DR_f \approx 21.3$ dB.
2.3.8 Hard Tier 1 §3.7 cascaded IP3 ? ✓
A two-stage cascade: stage 1 has OIP3$_{,1} = 20$ dBm, $G_1 = 10$ dB; stage 2 has OIP3$_{,2} = 25$ dBm, $G_2 = 20$ dB. Compute the cascaded OIP3 from
$\dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}} = \dfrac{1}{G_2 \cdot \text{OIP3}_{,1}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}_{,2}}$.
Which stage dominates? Contrast with noise figure where the first stage dominates.
2.3.9 Hard Tier 2 §3.7 3-stage cascade ? ✓
Three stages: LNA (G = 15 dB, OIP3 = 15 dBm), mixer (G = -7 dB, OIP3 = 18 dBm), IF amp (G = 25 dB, OIP3 = 28 dBm). Compute the cascaded OIP3 referred to the output. Which stage limits linearity?
2.3.10 Medium Tier 2 §3.4 IIP3 vs OIP3 ? ✓
For an amplifier with $G = 12$ dB and OIP3 $= 24$ dBm, compute IIP3. Then for $-20$ dBm input/tone, find the input-referred IM3 level using IIP3 (no need to convert to output).
2.3.11 Hard Tier 3 HW H4 ACPR ? ✓
For a digitally modulated signal (e.g., LTE OFDM) the IM "products" smear into the adjacent channel. Define ACPR (Adjacent Channel Power Ratio) and explain qualitatively why it tracks IMR (Intermodulation Ratio) at the same average power. Why does ACPR specification typically require 3–4 dB more OIP3 than a 2-tone IMR target?
2.3.12 Hard Tier 3 full receiver DR ? ✓
A full receiver has cascaded $F_{sys} = 4$ dB, $G_{sys} = 60$ dB, OIP3 $= 30$ dBm, $B = 1$ MHz. Compute (a) input-referred noise floor in dBm, (b) IIP3, (c) input-referred SFDR. Is the SFDR sufficient for handling a $-30$ dBm interferer alongside a $-90$ dBm desired signal?
2.3.13 Easy Tier 1 📖 From textbook §3.1 Taylor model ? ✓
A nonlinear amplifier is modeled as $v_o = 5\,v_i + 0.3\,v_i^2 - 0.05\,v_i^3$ (volts in & out). For a single-tone input $v_i(t) = 0.4\cos(\omega_0 t)$ V, expand $v_o(t)$ and identify (a) DC offset, (b) fundamental amplitude at $\omega_0$, (c) 2nd-harmonic amplitude at $2\omega_0$, (d) 3rd-harmonic amplitude at $3\omega_0$, (e) effective small-signal gain at the fundamental.
2.3.14 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §3.2 P1dB ? ✓
An amplifier has small-signal gain $G_{lin} = 18$ dB and output 1 dB compression point $P_{1dB,out} = 10$ dBm. (a) Sketch (or describe in words) $P_{out}$ vs. $P_{in}$ from $-30$ to $0$ dBm input. (b) Estimate the actual $P_{out}$ when $P_{in} = -8$ dBm. (c) Locate $P_{1dB,in}$.
2.3.15 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §3.3 IM products ? ✓
Two CW tones at $f_1 = 2.400$ GHz and $f_2 = 2.410$ GHz drive a nonlinear amplifier. (a) List the IM2 products and IM3 products (frequencies in MHz). (b) Which products fall inside a 100 MHz passband centered at 2.405 GHz? (c) For each in-band product, name the dominant Taylor coefficient ($a_2$ or $a_3$) responsible.
2.3.16 Medium Tier 1 📖 From textbook §3.4 IP3 ? ✓
Two-tone test: $P_{in,tone} = -10$ dBm per tone. Measured: fundamental output $P_{out,fund} = 0$ dBm (so gain $G = 10$ dB), IM3 product at $P_{IM3} = -50$ dBm. Compute (a) IIP3, (b) OIP3, (c) the IM3 ratio in dBc and dynamic-range implications.
2.3.17 Easy Tier 1 📖 From textbook §3.5 P1dB ↔ IP3 rule ? ✓
An amplifier has OIP3 $= 28$ dBm and gain $G = 25$ dB. Use the empirical rule $\text{OIP}_3 \approx OP_{1dB} + 10.6$ dB to estimate (a) $OP_{1dB}$, (b) $IP_{1dB,in}$, (c) IIP3. State one assumption / limitation of this rule.
2.3.18 Hard Tier 1 📖 From textbook §3.6 dynamic range ? ✓
Receiver: $B = 1$ MHz, $F = 4$ dB, $T_a = 290$ K, OIP3 $= 20$ dBm, $OP_{1dB} = 10$ dBm, gain $G = 30$ dB. Compute (a) output noise floor $N_o$ (dBm), (b) linear dynamic range $DR_l$ (dB), (c) spurious-free dynamic range $DR_f$ (dB), (d) verify the relation $DR_l \approx DR_f + 21$ dB.
2.3.19 Hard Tier 1 📖 From textbook §3.7 cascaded IP3 ? ✓
Two-stage chain: LNA ($G_1 = 20$ dB, OIP3$_1 = 15$ dBm) followed by mixer (loss $L = 6$ dB, OIP3$_2 = 20$ dBm). Compute the overall OIP3 referred to the cascade output. Which stage dominates linearity? Compare with the noise-figure cascade conclusion (which stage dominates NF) and discuss the design tension.
2.3.20 Medium Tier 2 📖 From textbook §3.8 PIM ? ✓
A cellular base-station transmits two carriers at 850 MHz and 855 MHz, each at $P_t = +43$ dBm into the antenna feedline. The combined antenna/duplexer has a measured PIM3 product of $-125$ dBm at the receive antenna port. (a) Find the PIM3 level relative to a single $+43$ dBm carrier (in dBc). (b) If the receiver noise floor is $-110$ dBm, by how much is the PIM3 below the noise floor? (c) Why is this typically a transmitter-side (not receiver-side) concern?
Total: 42 problems  ·  Tier 1 (must-do): 24  ·  Tier 2: 12  ·  Tier 3 (research): 6 0 done · 0 for review

Worked Solutions

Detailed worked solutions to all 42 problems, with formulas, substitutions, unit checks, and design interpretation. Double-click any problem above to jump to its solution; double-click any solution to jump back to the question.

Part 1 — Noise & BER

2.1.1noise sources

Thermal — thermal vibration of bound charges in any resistive element (resistors, lossy lines, atmosphere); white spectrum.
Shot — discrete charge carriers crossing a junction; $\propto I_{DC}$; dominant in diodes/transistors.
Flicker (1/f) — surface defects in semiconductors; sets oscillator phase-noise floor.
Plasma — random motion in ionized gas (atmosphere, sparking contacts).
Quantum — quantized photons; negligible at microwave because $hf \ll kT$.

In a discrete-component microwave receiver at room temperature, thermal noise dominates (everything is at $\sim 290$ K and resistive losses are unavoidable).
2.1.2thermal noise

(a) $v_n^s = \sqrt{4kTBR} = \sqrt{4(1.38\!\times\!10^{-23})(290)(10^7)(50)} = \sqrt{8.0\!\times\!10^{-12}} \approx 2.83\;\mu\text{V rms}$.

(b) $P_n = kTB = (1.38\!\times\!10^{-23})(290)(10^7) = 4.0\!\times\!10^{-14}\;\text{W}$.
In dBm: $10\log(4.0\!\times\!10^{-11}\;\text{mW}) = -104\;\text{dBm}$. Equivalently $-174 + 10\log(10^7) = -174+70 = -104$ dBm.

$v_n^s \approx 2.83\;\mu\text{V}$, $P_n = -104$ dBm
2.1.3kT₀ floor

$kT_0 = (1.38\!\times\!10^{-23})(290) = 4.00\!\times\!10^{-21}$ W/Hz $= 4.00\!\times\!10^{-18}$ mW/Hz.
$10\log(4.00\!\times\!10^{-18}) = -174$ dBm/Hz. ✓

Floor $= -174 + 10\log B$:

  • $B=1$ kHz → $-144$ dBm
  • $B=1$ MHz → $-114$ dBm
  • $B=1$ GHz → $-84$ dBm
No physical receiver at $T_0$ can detect a signal below this — it is the absolute thermal floor.
2.1.4PSD ↔ power

Two-sided $S(f) = n_0/2$ for all $f$. An ideal BPF passes $[-f_c-B/2, -f_c+B/2]$ and $[f_c-B/2, f_c+B/2]$.

$P_n = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(f)\,|H(f)|^2 df = \tfrac{n_0}{2}(B) + \tfrac{n_0}{2}(B) = n_0 B = kTB$.

The two halves account for negative and positive frequencies — both are physical (real-signal symmetry).

$P_n = n_0 B = kTB$
2.1.5white noise autocorrelation

Method

Use the Wiener-Khinchin theorem: autocorrelation and PSD are Fourier transform pairs. For ideal white noise the PSD is constant, so its inverse Fourier transform is an impulse.

$R(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S(f)\} = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{n_0/2\} = (n_0/2)\,\delta(\tau)$.

For $\tau \neq 0$: $R(\tau)=E[n(t)n(t+\tau)] = 0$ — distinct samples are uncorrelated.
At $\tau = 0$: $R(0) = \int S(f)\,df = \infty$ — infinite total power. White noise is a mathematical idealization; real noise is band-limited.

White noise is memoryless in time. Real receivers make total noise power finite by band-limiting the PSD.
2.1.6Parseval

Method

Start from the finite-energy Parseval relation, then use the power-signal version for stationary random noise.

For finite-energy signals: $\int|x(t)|^2 dt = \int|X(f)|^2 df$.
For random/power signals, $S(f)$ is the time-averaged $|X_T(f)|^2/T$ as $T\to\infty$.

Setting $\tau=0$: $R(0) = E[|n(t)|^2]$ — the mean-square value, i.e. the time-average power.
Wiener–Khinchin: $R(0) = \int S(f)\,df$. Hence $\int S(f)\,df$ is the total power.

For a receiver filter, include the filter response: $P_n=\int S(f)|H(f)|^2df$. With an ideal bandpass of physical bandwidth $B$ and two-sided $S(f)=n_0/2$, the positive and negative passbands contribute $n_0B/2+n_0B/2=n_0B$.

Parseval's role here is the bridge: area under PSD equals time-average noise power.
2.1.7SSB / DSB-SC SNR

Coherent demod: multiply by $2\cos\omega_c t$, lowpass.

SSB: Signal occupies one sideband only. Multiplication recovers baseband; noise in that single sideband is shifted to baseband. Both signal power and noise power transfer 1:1 → SNR$_{out}$ = SNR$_{in}$.

DSB-SC: Signal in both sidebands adds coherently after demod (signal voltage doubles → power $\times 4$). Noise in upper and lower sidebands is uncorrelated (white) so noise powers add (noise $\times 2$). Net SNR gain $= 4/2 = 2 = +3$ dB.

DSB-SC's 3 dB advantage = coherent signal addition vs incoherent noise addition.
2.1.8BER curve

Solve $P_e = \tfrac12\mathrm{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/n_0})$:

  • $P_e=10^{-3}$ → $\sqrt{E_b/n_0}\!\approx\!2.18$ → $E_b/n_0 \approx 4.75 = 6.8$ dB
  • $P_e=10^{-5}$ → $\sqrt{E_b/n_0}\!\approx\!3.0$ → $E_b/n_0 = 9.0 \approx 9.6$ dB
  • $P_e=10^{-9}$ → $\sqrt{E_b/n_0}\!\approx\!4.27$ → $E_b/n_0 \approx 18.2 = 12.6$ dB
A 3 dB increase in $E_b/n_0$ near threshold drops BER by ~3 orders of magnitude — the "waterfall" curve.
2.1.9integrate-and-dump

Input: $\pm V\cos\omega_c t + n(t)$ over $[0,T]$. Multiply by $\cos\omega_c t$, integrate.

(a) Signal: $\int_0^T (\pm V)\cos^2\omega_c t\,dt = \pm V T/2$ (peak $\omega_c T \gg 1$ assumption).

(b) Noise variance: $\sigma^2 = E\!\left[\left(\!\int_0^T n(t)\cos\omega_c t\,dt\right)^{\!2}\right] = \int_0^T\!\!\int_0^T R_n(t-s)\cos\omega_c t\cos\omega_c s\,ds\,dt$.
With $R_n(\tau)=(n_0/2)\delta(\tau)$: $\sigma^2 = (n_0/2)\int_0^T\cos^2\omega_c t\,dt = n_0 T/4$.
Some books use the convention $\sigma^2_{out} = n_0/T$ after normalizing by $T$.

(c) SNR: Signal power $(VT/2)^2 = V^2T^2/4$; noise power $n_0 T/4$. SNR $= V^2 T / n_0 = 2E_b/n_0$ where $E_b = V^2T/2$.

Matched-filter SNR $= 2E_b/n_0$ — the optimum for AWGN.
2.1.10AM efficiency

$\eta = m^2/(2+m^2)$:

  • $m=0.3$: $0.09/2.09 = 4.3\%$
  • $m=0.5$: $0.25/2.25 = 11.1\%$
  • $m=0.7$: $0.49/2.49 = 19.7\%$
  • $m=1.0$: $1/3 = 33.3\%$
Even at full modulation, $\ge 67\%$ of transmitted power sits in the carrier and conveys no info — DSB-SC and SSB beat DSB-LC for power efficiency.
2.1.11modulation thresholds

$E_b/n_0$ (dB) at BER $= 10^{-5}$:

  • Coh BPSK: 9.6 ← best
  • Coh FSK: 12.6
  • Noncoh FSK: 13.4
  • Coh ASK: 15.6
  • Noncoh ASK: 16.4 ← worst
Best vs worst gap $\approx 6.8$ dB. Coherent BPSK is the most power-efficient binary modulation; noncoherent ASK is the least.
2.1.12spectral efficiency

$\eta_s = \log_2 M$ (bps/Hz, ideal Nyquist):

  • BPSK: 1, QPSK: 2, 8-PSK: 3, 16-QAM: 4, 64-QAM: 6

Need $\eta_s \ge 24/6 = 4$ bps/Hz.

Lowest order that fits: 16-QAM (exactly 4 bps/Hz). 8-PSK gives only 3 — insufficient.
2.1.13QPSK link min SNR

$S/N|_{dB} = (E_b/n_0)|_{dB} + 10\log(R_b/B)$.

$10\log(48.6/30) = 10\log(1.62) = 2.10$ dB.

$S/N_{min} = 9.6 + 2.10 = 11.7$ dB
2.1.1416-QAM

(a) For 16-QAM at $R_b/B = 12/4 = 3$:
$S/N = 14.4 + 10\log 3 = 14.4 + 4.77 = 19.2$ dB.

(b) QPSK at the same $R_b=12$ Mbps needs $B = 6$ MHz (since $\eta_s = 2$):
$S/N = 9.6 + 10\log(12/6) = 9.6 + 3.01 = 12.6$ dB.

16-QAM penalty: $19.2 - 12.6 = 6.6$ dB more SNR — but it uses half the bandwidth.
2.1.15📖 noise sources

(a) GaAs HEMT LNA at 4 GHz: thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise from the channel resistance dominates; shot noise of the gate is negligible because gate current is essentially zero in a HEMT.

(b) Si BJT mixer at 100 MHz, low $I_C$: shot noise of the base/emitter junctions dominates because thermal noise of the small-signal $r_e$ is comparable but the $2qI$ shot term wins at low currents and low frequency.

(c) Varactor multiplier at 8 GHz: thermal noise of the series resistance and the lossy substrate; the device is essentially passive (no DC bias current → no shot noise).

Rule of thumb: any device with significant DC current → shot noise; any passive/lossy element → thermal; flicker only matters below ~MHz; plasma/quantum only at extremes.
2.1.16📖 thermal noise

(a) $v_n^{rms} = \sqrt{4kTRB} = \sqrt{4(1.38\!\times\!10^{-23})(300)(2200)(5\!\times\!10^6)} \approx 6.0\ \mu$V.

(b) Available noise power $P_n = kTB = (1.38\!\times\!10^{-23})(300)(5\!\times\!10^6) = 2.07\!\times\!10^{-14}$ W $= -106.8$ dBm.

(c) At 77 K, $v_n^{rms}$ scales as $\sqrt{T}$: factor $\sqrt{77/300} = 0.507$, so new value $\approx 3.0\ \mu$V (and noise power drops by $10\log(77/300) = -5.9$ dB).

Cooling helps because $v_n \propto \sqrt{T}$ — a 4× drop in temperature halves the noise voltage.
2.1.17📖 PSD ↔ power

For an ideal first-order RC LPF, the noise bandwidth is $B_n = (\pi/2) f_c = 1.571\times f_c = 157.1$ MHz.

Total output noise voltage variance: $\sigma_v^2 = 2 S_n B_n$ (factor 2 because two-sided integrates positive + negative frequencies):
$\sigma_v^2 = 2 \times 2\!\times\!10^{-18} \times 1.571\!\times\!10^8 = 6.28\!\times\!10^{-10}$ V$^2$.

$v_{rms} = \sqrt{\sigma_v^2} = 25.1\ \mu$V across 50 $\Omega$.

Noise power into 50 $\Omega$: $P_n = v_{rms}^2 / 50 = 1.26\!\times\!10^{-11}$ W $= -79.0$ dBm.

$B_n = (\pi/2) f_c$ is the equivalent rectangular noise bandwidth of any 1-pole RC filter — $57\%$ wider than the 3 dB cutoff.
2.1.18📖 SSB SNR

Signal power: $S_i = (1\!\times\!10^{-3})^2 / 50 = 2\!\times\!10^{-8}$ W $= -47.0$ dBm.

Noise power across 30 kHz: $N_i = -174 + 10\log(30\!\times\!10^3) = -174 + 44.8 = -129.2$ dBm.

(a) SNR$_i = -47 - (-129.2) = 82.2$ dB.

(b) For a coherent SSB demodulator the output SNR equals the input SNR within a constant; SNR$_o = 82.2$ dB.

(c) Improvement = 0 dB. SSB is a linear demodulator — no SNR gain, but also no penalty (unlike envelope-detected AM, which has a threshold).

SSB exchanges bandwidth (half of DSB-AM) for the same SNR performance and avoids the $\sim 3$ dB AM threshold.
2.1.19📖 BER

BPSK at $E_b/n_0 = 7$ dB: linear $E_b/n_0 = 5.01$. $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2\times 5.01}) = Q(3.17) \approx 7.6\!\times\!10^{-4}$.

64-QAM at the same $E_b/n_0$: required $E_b/n_0$ for $P_b = 10^{-6}$ is $\approx 18.5$ dB; at 7 dB the BER is essentially $> 10^{-1}$ (deep below threshold).

Quantitatively, for square M-QAM with Gray coding: $P_b \approx \frac{4}{\log_2 M}\Big(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\Big) Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3\log_2 M}{M-1}\,\frac{E_b}{n_0}}\right)$. Plug $M=64$, $E_b/n_0 = 5.01$ → argument $\sqrt{(3\cdot 6/63)\cdot 5.01} = \sqrt{1.43} = 1.20$, so $Q(1.2) \approx 0.115$ and $P_b \approx 0.115$.

BPSK at 7 dB → BER $\sim 10^{-3}$ (usable with FEC). 64-QAM at 7 dB → BER $\sim 0.1$ (unusable). 64-QAM needs $\sim 11$ dB more SNR for the same BER.
2.1.20📖 integrate-and-dump

(a) Matched filter (integrate-and-dump): noise bandwidth equals signal bandwidth; $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2\, E_b/n_0}) = Q(\sqrt{2\times 7.94}) = Q(3.99) \approx 3.4\!\times\!10^{-5}$.

(b) Brick-wall LPF at $B = 1.5$ MHz (excess bandwidth $50\%$): noise power is $1.5\times$ that of the matched filter, so effective $E_b/n_0$ drops by $10\log(1.5) = 1.76$ dB. New $E_b/n_0 = 9 - 1.76 = 7.24$ dB → $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2\times 5.30}) = Q(3.26) \approx 5.6\!\times\!10^{-4}$.

Matched filter wins because it integrates exactly the signal energy and rejects noise outside the signal-energy bandwidth — any wider filter just lets in more noise.
2.1.21📖 modulation comparison
Modulation$E_b/n_0$ for $10^{-6}$$\eta_s$ (max bps/Hz)Penalty vs BPSKUsed in
BPSK10.5 dB10 dBsatellite TT&C
QPSK10.5 dB20 dBDVB-S, GPS
8-PSK14.0 dB3+3.5 dBDVB-S2 (some MODCODs)
16-QAM14.4 dB4+3.9 dBWi-Fi, LTE, 5G NR
64-QAM18.6 dB6+8.1 dBWi-Fi 5/6, LTE, 5G NR
256-QAM23.5 dB8+13.0 dBWi-Fi 6/7, 5G mmWave
Doubling spectral efficiency costs $\sim 4$ dB SNR; this is the bandwidth-vs-power trade-off at the heart of modulation choice.
2.1.22📖 16-QAM

(a) Symbol rate $R_s = R_b / \log_2 M = 80 / 4 = 20$ Msym/s. Spectral efficiency $\eta_s = R_b / B = 80 / 25 = 3.2$ bps/Hz.

(b) From Pozar Table 14.1 / standard curves: 16-QAM BER $= 10^{-5}$ → $E_b/n_0 \approx 13.4$ dB.

(c) $S/N = E_b/n_0 + 10\log(R_b/B) = 13.4 + 10\log 3.2 = 13.4 + 5.05 = 18.5$ dB.

(d) Noise floor $N = kT_{sys}B = (1.38\!\times\!10^{-23})(400)(25\!\times\!10^6) = 1.38\!\times\!10^{-13}$ W $= -98.6$ dBm. Required $P_r = N + (S/N) = -98.6 + 18.5 = -80.1$ dBm.

A $-80$ dBm signal in 25 MHz at $T_{sys} = 400$ K supports 16-QAM at $10^{-5}$ — the typical Wi-Fi MCS-3 / LTE PDSCH operating point.

Part 2 — Noise Figure, Temperature, Antenna & Link Budget

2.2.1F ↔ Tₑ

$T_e = (F-1)T_0$, $T_0 = 290$ K:

  • $F = 1$ dB ($1.259$) → $T_e = 75.1$ K
  • $F = 3$ dB ($1.995$) → $T_e \approx 289$ K
  • $F = 6$ dB ($3.98$) → $T_e \approx 864$ K
  • $F = 10$ dB ($10$) → $T_e = 2610$ K

$F = 1 + T_e/T_0$:

  • $T_e=50$ → $F=1.172 = 0.69$ dB
  • $T_e=170$ → $F=1.586 = 2.00$ dB
  • $T_e=580$ → $F=3.0 = 4.77$ dB
  • $T_e=2610$ → $F=10 = 10$ dB
2.2.2amplifier Tₑ from gain

Output noise added (source at 0 K) $= G\,k T_e B$. Solve:

$T_e = N_{add}/(GkB) = (4\!\times\!10^{-13})/(100 \cdot 1.38\!\times\!10^{-23} \cdot 10^8) = 4\!\times\!10^{-13}/1.38\!\times\!10^{-13} \approx 2.9$ K.

$F = 1 + T_e/T_0 = 1 + 2.9/290 \approx 1.010 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.04$ dB.

$T_e \approx 2.9$ K, $F \approx 0.04$ dB — characteristic of a cryogenically cooled radio-astronomy LNA.
2.2.3Y-factor

$T_e = (T_h - YT_c)/(Y-1) = (873 - 2.5\!\cdot\!77)/(2.5-1) = (873 - 192.5)/1.5 = 680.5/1.5$.

$T_e = 454$ K. $F = 1 + 454/290 = 2.56$.

$T_e \approx 454$ K, $F \approx 4.08$ dB
2.2.4lossy line at T₀

$L = 10^{0.25} = 1.778$. At $T_p = T_0$: $F = L = 2.5$ dB exactly.

$T_e = (L-1)T_p = 0.778 \cdot 290 = 226$ K.

Why $F = L$: at $T_p = T_0$, both source noise and added noise are $kT_0B$. Power gets attenuated by $L$ but added noise refills to keep output noise at $kT_0B$ → SNR drops by exactly $L$.

Equivalently, use the temperature form: $F=1+T_e/T_0=1+(L-1)T_0/T_0=L$. This is why a passive room-temperature loss has noise figure equal to insertion loss.

A 2.5 dB line at $T_0$ contributes $T_e=226$ K and has $F=2.5$ dB.
2.2.5cold lossy line

$L = 10^{0.3} = 2.0$. At $T_p = 200$ K: $T_e = (L-1)T_p = 1\cdot 200 = 200$ K.

If naively $T_p = 290$: $T_e = 1\cdot 290 = 290$ K.

Cooling the line from 290 → 200 K cuts $T_e$ by 90 K. Cold lossy components add proportionally less noise — that's why deep-space feeds are cryocooled.
2.2.6Friis cascade

Linear: $F_1=1.585$, $G_1=31.62$; $F_2=5.01$, $G_2=0.251$; $F_3=2.512$.

$F = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + (F_3-1)/(G_1 G_2)$
$= 1.585 + 4.01/31.62 + 1.512/(31.62\cdot 0.251)$
$= 1.585 + 0.127 + 1.512/7.93 = 1.585 + 0.127 + 0.190 = 1.902$.

$F = 1.90 \Rightarrow 2.79$ dB; $T_e = (F-1)T_0 = 0.902 \cdot 290 \approx 261$ K.
2.2.7loss-before-LNA penalty

(a) Clean: $F_1=1.585\;(2\,\text{dB})$, $G_1=100$; $F_2=6.31\;(8\,\text{dB})$, $G_2=0.251$; $F_3=2.512\;(4\,\text{dB})$.
$F_a = 1.585 + 5.31/100 + 1.512/(100\cdot 0.251) = 1.585 + 0.053 + 0.060 = 1.698 \Rightarrow 2.30$ dB.

(b) With cable: add 1.5 dB cable ($F_0 = L = 1.413$, $G_0 = 0.708$) at front:
$F_b = 1.413 + (1.585-1)/0.708 + 5.31/(0.708\cdot 100) + 1.512/(0.708\cdot 100\cdot 0.251)$
$= 1.413 + 0.826 + 0.075 + 0.085 = 2.40 \Rightarrow 3.80$ dB.

Penalty $= 3.80 - 2.30 = 1.50$ dB — exactly the cable loss. Design rule: any loss before the LNA degrades NF dB-for-dB.
2.2.8Wilkinson

A matched, lossless Wilkinson splits input power equally to two output ports — each port sees $P_{in}/2$, i.e. 3 dB lower.

From one output port's perspective, the divider looks like a 3 dB lossy two-port (input → that output), so by the lossy-line rule, $F = L = 3$ dB at $T_p = T_0$.

No contradiction: the "missing" half-power didn't dissipate — it went out the other output port. The receiver at port 2 still sees its input SNR halved.

Detailed SNR view: for one branch, the desired signal available at the receiver is halved. The source noise delivered into that branch is also halved, but the branch still sees a matched $T_0$ environment. Referred to a single output path, the available SNR is degraded by the same factor as a 3 dB attenuator.

For each output branch, a lossless Wilkinson behaves like a 3 dB passive loss for noise-figure accounting.
2.2.9antenna noise temp

$T_a = \eta_{rad}T_b + (1-\eta_{rad})T_p = 0.9\cdot 50 + 0.1\cdot 290 = 45 + 29 = 74$ K.

Pointed at the ground ($T_b = 290$): $T_a = 0.9\cdot 290 + 0.1\cdot 290 = 290$ K.

Cold sky → $T_a$ stays low (74 K). Warm scene → $T_a$ approaches physical temp (290 K) regardless of efficiency.
2.2.10EIRP & FSPL

(a) EIRP $= P_t + G_t = 10\log(100) + 35 = 20 + 35 = 55$ dBW $= 85$ dBm.

(b) $\lambda = 3\!\times\!10^8/12\!\times\!10^9 = 0.025$ m.
FSPL $= 32.4 + 20\log(12000) + 20\log(36000) = 32.4 + 81.6 + 91.1 \approx 205$ dB.

(c) $P_r = $ EIRP $- $ FSPL $+ G_r = 55 - 205 + 30 = -120$ dBW $= -90$ dBm.

2.2.11G/T

$G/T = G_r - 10\log(T_a + T_e) = 30 - 10\log(80+120) = 30 - 10\log 200 = 30 - 23.0 = 7.0$ dB/K.

With $T_e \to 60$ K: $G/T = 30 - 10\log(140) = 30 - 21.46 = 8.54$ dB/K.

Improvement = $1.5$ dB. Lowering $T_e$ by 60 K matters because $T_a$ is already comparable; if $T_a \gg T_e$, the LNA upgrade buys little.
2.2.12DBS link CNR

$10\log(kB) = -228.6 + 10\log(2.7\!\times\!10^7) = -228.6 + 74.3 = -154.3$ dBW/Hz·Hz.

CNR $= $ EIRP $+ G/T - $ FSPL $- 10\log(kB)$
$= 55 + 12 - 205 - (-154.3) = 55 + 12 - 205 + 154.3 = 16.3$ dB.

QPSK at BER $=10^{-6}$ needs $E_b/n_0 \approx 10.5$ dB; with $R_b/B=2$ → $S/N_{req} \approx 13.5$ dB.

CNR = 16.3 dB > 13.5 dB → link closes with 2.8 dB margin.
2.2.13minimum detectable

$F = 2$ ($3$ dB), so $(F-1)T_0 = 290$ K. Effective noise temp at antenna port $= T_a + (F-1)T_0 = 100 + 290 = 390$ K.

$S_{i,min} = kB\cdot 390 \cdot 10 = (1.38\!\times\!10^{-23})(10^6)(390)(10) = 5.4\!\times\!10^{-14}$ W $= -132.7$ dBm.

Why not $kT_0BF$? $kT_0BF \cdot (S_o/N_o) = (1.38e^{-23})(290)(10^6)(2)(10) = 8.0e^{-14}$ W $= -130.9$ dBm — about 1.8 dB too high. Because the antenna is cold ($T_a = 100$ K $\ne T_0$), the standard formula overstates the noise.

2.2.14full receiver Tₛᵧₛ

$L_T = 10^{0.15} = 1.413$.
(a) $T_{TL} = (L_T-1)T_p = 0.413\cdot 290 = 119.7$ K.
$T_{REC} = (F-1)T_0 = (1.585-1)(290) = 169.7$ K.

(b) $T_{SYS} = T_a + T_{TL} + L_T\cdot T_{REC} = 60 + 119.7 + 1.413\cdot 169.7 = 60 + 119.7 + 239.8 = 419.5$ K.

(c) Equivalent system NF: $F_{sys} = 1 + T_{SYS}/T_0 = 1 + 419.5/290 = 2.45 \Rightarrow 3.89$ dB.

2.2.15Pozar 14.6

$L_T = 10^{0.2} = 1.585$, $F = 10^{0.4} = 2.512$.
$T_{TL} = 0.585\cdot 270 = 158.0$ K; $T_{REC} = 1.512\cdot 290 = 438.5$ K.

$T_{SYS} = 200 + 158.0 + 1.585\cdot 438.5 = 200 + 158 + 695 = 1053$ K.
$F_{sys} = 1 + 1053/290 = 4.63 \Rightarrow 6.65$ dB.

Sanity check at $T_a = T_0 = 290$ K:
$T_{SYS} = 290 + 158 + 695 = 1143$ K, $F_{sys} = 1 + 1143/290 = 4.94 \Rightarrow 6.94$ dB.
Friis (line + receiver): $F_{line+rec} = L_T \cdot F = 1.585\cdot 2.512 = 3.98 \Rightarrow 6.0$ dB. With $T_p = 270 \neq T_0$ the equality is approximate; if we set $T_p = T_0$ also, $T_{SYS}$ would be exactly $T_0\cdot F_{tot}$.

2.2.16design exercise

Open-ended; one workable budget for 64-QAM, $R_b = 100$ Mbps, BER $=10^{-6}$ ($E_b/n_0 = 22.5$ dB):

  • $\eta_s \approx 5$ bps/Hz (with rolloff) → $B \approx 20$ MHz
  • Required $S/N = 22.5 + 10\log(100/20) = 22.5 + 7 = 29.5$ dB; aim for $\ge 32.5$ dB with 3 dB margin
  • Try Ku-band: $f=20$ GHz, $R=36{,}000$ km → FSPL $\approx 209.6$ dB
  • $P_t = 50$ W (47 dBm), $G_t = 40$ dBi → EIRP $= 87$ dBm $= 57$ dBW
  • $G_r = 50$ dBi (dish), $T_a = 30$ K, $T_e = 70$ K (cooled LNA) → $G/T = 50 - 10\log(100) = 30$ dB/K
  • $10\log(kB) = -228.6 + 73 = -155.6$ dBW/Hz
  • CNR $= 57 + 30 - 209.6 + 155.6 = 33.0$ dB → margin $= 33.0 - 29.5 = 3.5$ dB ✓
Bottleneck: 64-QAM is SNR-hungry — every dB of $S/N$ shortfall must be absorbed by larger antenna or higher $P_t$. Fall back to 16-QAM if $E_b/n_0$ headroom shrinks.
2.2.17📖 equivalent Tₑ

(a) $F = 2.5$ dB → linear $F = 10^{0.25} = 1.778$. $T_e = (F-1)T_0 = 0.778 \times 290 = 225.6$ K.

(b) New source $T_s = 100$ K → $T_{sys} = 100 + 225.6 = 325.6$ K. Effective $F$ at this source temperature: $F_{eff} = 1 + T_e/T_s = 1 + 225.6/100 = 3.256 = 5.13$ dB.

Lesson: the IEEE noise figure is referenced to $T_0 = 290$ K. When the source is colder, the device's noise looks worse in NF terms because $T_e$ is now a larger fraction of the total — even though the absolute noise added is unchanged.
2.2.18📖 Y-factor

$Y = (T_h + T_e) / (T_c + T_e) = 1.45$
$\Rightarrow T_e = (T_h - Y T_c) / (Y - 1) = (470 - 1.45 \times 80) / 0.45 = 354/0.45 = 786.7$ K.

$F = 1 + T_e/T_0 = 1 + 786.7/290 = 3.71 = 5.69$ dB.

Y-factor with cold-load < $T_0$ gives a noise-source ENR (excess noise ratio) handle to extract $T_e$ directly without needing $T_0 = 290$ K.
2.2.19📖 noise figure

(a) Input-referred noise: $kT_0BF = -174 + 10\log(10^7) + 4 = -174 + 70 + 4 = -100$ dBm.

(b) Output noise: $G + (-100) = 30 + (-100) = -70$ dBm.

(c) SNR$_i = -80 - (-100) = 20$ dB. Output: $S_o = -80 + 30 = -50$ dBm; SNR$_o = -50 - (-70) = 20$ dB.

Noise figure $F$ is, by definition, the SNR degradation; here SNR$_i$ = SNR$_o$ + 4 dB (the $F$ dB).
2.2.20📖 lossy line

(a) Passive line at $T_p = T_0$: $F_{line} = L = 2$ dB → linear $1.585$. $T_{line} = (L-1)T_p = 0.585 \times 290 = 169.7$ K.

(b) LNA: $F_{LNA} = 1.5$ dB = 1.413, $T_{LNA} = (1.413-1)\times 290 = 119.8$ K. Cascade with line first ($G_1 = 1/L = 0.631$):
$T_{cas} = T_{line} + T_{LNA}/G_1 = 169.7 + 119.8/0.631 = 169.7 + 189.8 = 359.5$ K.
$F_{cas} = 1 + 359.5/290 = 2.24 = 3.50$ dB.

(c) Sensitivity penalty: $F_{cas} - F_{LNA} = 3.50 - 1.5 = 2.0$ dB — exactly the line loss (this is the universal "every dB of pre-LNA loss costs 1 dB of NF" rule).

Pre-LNA loss is the most expensive loss in a receiver — minimize cable length, lossy filters, switches before the LNA.
2.2.21📖 Friis cascade

Linear gains/figures: $G_1 = 31.6$, $F_1 = 1.585$ (T₁ = 169.7 K); $L_2 = 3.98$, $G_2 = 1/3.98 = 0.251$, $F_2 = 6.31$ (T₂ = 1540 K); $G_3 = 1000$, $F_3 = 1.585$ (T₃ = 169.7 K).

$F_{cas} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + (F_3-1)/(G_1 G_2) = 1.585 + 5.31/31.6 + 0.585/(31.6\times 0.251)$
$= 1.585 + 0.168 + 0.0738 = 1.827 = 2.62$ dB.

LNA noise figure (2 dB) dominates; mixer's 8 dB NF gets divided by $G_1 = 31.6$ → contributes only 0.17 to total $F$. Friis: first stage rules.
2.2.22📖 Wilkinson

An ideal Wilkinson is lossless (3 dB split is just power division, not insertion loss). The 0.3 dB excess loss per arm is the actual insertion loss seen by signal and noise alike.

From one output port: looking back, the device behaves as a 3 dB attenuator (split) + 0.3 dB extra loss = 3.3 dB total loss at $T_p = 290$ K.

$T_e = (L - 1) T_p = (10^{0.33} - 1) \times 290 = (2.14 - 1) \times 290 = 330.5$ K.

$F = L = 3.3$ dB per arm.

For a passive equal-split divider at room temperature, NF (per arm) = total insertion loss in dB.
2.2.23📖 antenna Tₐ & Friis tx

(a) $T_a = \eta_{rad} T_{sky} + (1-\eta_{rad}) T_p = 0.85 \times 20 + 0.15 \times 290 = 17 + 43.5 = 60.5$ K.

(b) $T_{sys} = T_a + T_e = 60.5 + 60 = 120.5$ K.

(c) $N = kT_{sys}B = 1.38\!\times\!10^{-23} \times 120.5 \times 36\!\times\!10^6 = 5.99\!\times\!10^{-14}$ W $= -132.2$ dBm.

Antenna ohmic loss $(1-\eta_{rad}) T_p = 43.5$ K dominates over actual sky noise — the radome and feed losses are the noise budget bottleneck for cold-sky pointing.
2.2.24📖 G/T figure of merit

(a) $G/T = G_r - 10\log T_{sys} = 45 - 10\log 120 = 45 - 20.79 = 24.2$ dB/K.

(b) Link budget in dB:
$C/N_0 = $ EIRP $-$ FSPL $+ G/T - 10\log k$
$= 50 - 205 + 24.2 - (-228.6) = 97.8$ dB-Hz.

(c) For e.g. a 36 MHz transponder: $C/N = C/N_0 - 10\log B = 97.8 - 75.6 = 22.2$ dB — comfortably enough for 16-APSK / DVB-S2.

$G/T$ collapses two parameters into one figure of merit — the higher, the more sensitive the earth station.
2.2.25📖 full receiver

$T_{TL} = (L_T - 1)T_p$ with $L_T = 10^{0.05} = 1.122$ → $T_{TL} = 0.122 \times 290 = 35.4$ K.

$T_{SYS} = T_a + T_{TL} + L_T \cdot T_{REC} = 200 + 35.4 + 1.122 \times 180 = 200 + 35.4 + 202 = 437.4$ K.

$N_{SYS} = kT_{SYS}B = 1.38\!\times\!10^{-23}\times 437.4 \times 2\!\times\!10^7 = 1.21\!\times\!10^{-13}$ W $= -99.2$ dBm.

$\text{SNR}_{out} = S_i - N_{SYS} = -80 - (-99.2) = 19.2$ dB.

Sufficient for 16-QAM at BER $10^{-5}$ ($\sim 19$ dB needed). 64-QAM would require $\sim 5$ dB more — not enough margin.

Part 3 — Distortion (IP3, P1dB, DR)

2.3.1Taylor model

Substitute $v_i = A\cos\omega t$:

  • $a_1 v_i = a_1 A\cos\omega t$ → linear gain
  • $a_2 v_i^2 = a_2 A^2(\tfrac12 + \tfrac12\cos 2\omega t)$ → DC offset + 2nd harmonic
  • $a_3 v_i^3 = a_3 A^3(\tfrac34\cos\omega t + \tfrac14\cos 3\omega t)$ → fundamental gets a contribution $\tfrac{3a_3 A^3}{4}\cos\omega t$ (compression / expansion) and $\tfrac{a_3 A^3}{4}\cos 3\omega t$ (3rd harmonic)
For compression: $a_1 > 0$, $a_3 < 0$ (so the cubic term subtracts from the fundamental as $A$ grows).
2.3.2P1dB

Method

First compute the output predicted by the small-signal straight-line gain, then subtract the 1 dB compression error.

Small-signal extrapolated output at $P_{in}=-10$ dBm is $P_{out,ideal}=P_{in}+G=-10+20=10$ dBm.

At the 1 dB compression point, the actual output is 1 dB lower than this extrapolated value: $\text{OP1dB}=10-1=9$ dBm.

The "$-1$" subtracts 1 dB because at this point the gain has already compressed by 1 dB — output is 1 dB below the small-signal extrapolation.
2.3.3IM products

Set $\Delta = f_2 - f_1 = 1$ MHz.

2nd-order: $2f_1 \approx 2$ GHz, $2f_2 \approx 2$ GHz, $f_1 + f_2 \approx 2$ GHz, $f_2 - f_1 = 1$ MHz — all out-of-band.

3rd-order: $3f_1, 3f_2 \approx 3$ GHz (out); $2f_1 \pm f_2$ → $0.999, 3.001$ GHz; $2f_2 \pm f_1$ → $1.002, 3.002$ GHz.

In-band: $2f_1-f_2 = 0.999$ GHz and $2f_2-f_1 = 1.002$ GHz — only $\Delta$ away from $f_1, f_2$ → cannot be filtered out. That's why IM3 is the linearity-limiting product.
2.3.4OIP3 from two-tone

$\Delta P = P_{fund} - P_{IM3} = -10 - (-70) = 60$ dB.
OIP3 $= P_{fund} + \Delta P/2 = -10 + 30 = 20$ dBm.

Slope rule: on a $\log P_{out}$ vs $\log P_{in}$ plot, fund grows 1 dB / dB; IM3 grows 3 dB / dB. They intersect at IP3. At $\Delta$ dB backoff from IP3, fund is $\Delta$ dB below IP3 and IM3 is $3\Delta$ dB below — gap $= 2\Delta$.

Here the measured gap is 60 dB, so the fundamental is $60/2=30$ dB below the intercept. Since the measured fundamental is $-10$ dBm, the intercept is $-10+30=20$ dBm.

OIP3 $=20$ dBm.
2.3.5IM3 level

(a) $P_{out,fund} = P_{in} + G = -30 + 20 = -10$ dBm/tone.

(b) $P_{IM3} = 3 P_{out} - 2\,\text{OIP3} = 3(-10) - 2(30) = -30 - 60 = -90$ dBm.

Backoff check: the fundamental is $30-(-10)=40$ dB below OIP3. IM3 is twice that gap below the fundamental, so IM3 should be $2\cdot40=80$ dBc. $-10-80=-90$ dBm, matching the formula.

IM3 is $80$ dB below the fundamental — easily detectable in a spec-an, but masks weak signals.
2.3.6OIP3 ↔ OP1dB

(a) OP1dB $= 22$ → OIP3 $\approx 22 + 10.6 = 32.6$ dBm.

(b) OIP3 $= 35$ → OP1dB $\approx 35 - 10.6 = 24.4$ dBm.

(c) Breakdown cases:

  • Saturating PAs (Class C/E/F): output clips abruptly, OP1dB much closer to OIP3.
  • Strong even-order distortion: 10.6 dB rule assumes pure 3rd-order — fails when $a_2$-driven products dominate.
  • Heavy back-off / pre-distortion linearizers can push the gap to 12–15 dB.
2.3.7dynamic range

(a) $DR_l = $ OP1dB $- N_o = 20 - (-90) = 110$ dB.

(b) $DR_f = (2/3)($ OIP3 $- N_o) = (2/3)(120) = 80$ dB.

(c) $DR_l - DR_f = 30$ dB. The "$\approx 21.3$ dB" rule from §3.6 holds when OIP3 $-$ OP1dB $= 10.6$ dB and $N_o$ tracks the canonical $kT_0 B F$ floor; here OIP3 $-$ OP1dB $= 10$ dB and $N_o$ was specified independently, so the gap differs slightly from the rule.

2.3.8cascaded IP3 (2-stage)

Convert: OIP3$_{,1} = 20$ dBm $= 100$ mW, $G_1 = 10$ dB $=10$, $G_2 = 20$ dB $=100$, OIP3$_{,2} = 25$ dBm $= 316$ mW.

$\dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}} = \dfrac{1}{G_2 \cdot \text{OIP3}_{,1}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}_{,2}} = \dfrac{1}{100\cdot 100} + \dfrac{1}{316} = 10^{-4} + 3.16\!\times\!10^{-3} = 3.26\!\times\!10^{-3}$.

OIP3 $= 307$ mW $= 24.9$ dBm. Stage 2 dominates ($1/316$ term is 31× larger).

Last stage limits IP3 — opposite of noise figure where the first stage dominates. Receiver designers face the tension: low-noise (LNA first) vs high-linearity (best linearity at the back).
2.3.93-stage cascade

Linear: $G_1=31.62$, $G_2=0.200$, $G_3=316.2$. OIP3$_{,1}=31.6$ mW, OIP3$_{,2}=63.1$ mW, OIP3$_{,3}=631$ mW.

$\dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}_{,1} G_2 G_3} + \dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}_{,2} G_3} + \dfrac{1}{\text{OIP3}_{,3}}$
$= \dfrac{1}{31.6 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 316.2} + \dfrac{1}{63.1 \cdot 316.2} + \dfrac{1}{631}$
$= \dfrac{1}{1998} + \dfrac{1}{19952} + \dfrac{1}{631} = 5.0\!\times\!10^{-4} + 5.0\!\times\!10^{-5} + 1.585\!\times\!10^{-3} = 2.14\!\times\!10^{-3}$.

OIP3 $= 467$ mW $= 26.7$ dBm.

IF amp dominates ($1/631$ is the largest term). LNA's apparent OIP3 is amplified by $G_2 G_3$ to look much larger from the output — that's why the LNA contribution is small.
2.3.10IIP3

IIP3 $= $ OIP3 $- G = 24 - 12 = 12$ dBm.

Input-referred: $P_{IM3,in} = 3 P_{in} - 2\,\text{IIP3} = 3(-20) - 2(12) = -60 - 24 = -84$ dBm.

(Sanity-check at output: $P_{out,fund} = -8$ dBm, $P_{out,IM3} = 3(-8) - 2(24) = -72$ dBm = $-84 + G$ ✓.)

2.3.11ACPR vs IMR

ACPR = $10\log\dfrac{P_{adj\,channel}}{P_{main\,channel}}$ for a digitally modulated test signal — power that "leaks" into a defined adjacent channel BW.

For weakly-nonlinear amps the ACPR floor is set by 3rd-order products generated by all the in-band tones convolving with each other. Same physics as 2-tone IMR but with a continuum of tones.

Empirically, the ACPR test "sees" the integrated 3rd-order power vs 2-tone's peak product, so ACPR specs typically demand $\sim 3$–$4$ dB more OIP3 than the equivalent 2-tone IMR target.

2.3.12full receiver SFDR

(a) Input noise floor: $N_i = kT_0 B F = -174 + 60 + 4 = -110$ dBm. Output: $N_o = N_i + G = -110 + 60 = -50$ dBm.

(b) IIP3 $= $ OIP3 $- G = 30 - 60 = -30$ dBm.

(c) Input-referred SFDR $= (2/3)($ IIP3 $- N_i) = (2/3)(-30 - (-110)) = (2/3)(80) = 53.3$ dB.

Interferer scenario: $-30$ dBm interferer is right at IIP3 — IM3 generated will be $3(-30) - 2(-30) = -30$ dBm at input… that's the IP3 itself, completely overwhelming a $-90$ dBm desired signal. The receiver fails: gap $= -30 - (-90) = 60$ dB > SFDR (53.3 dB).

Need either a pre-LNA filter to attenuate the interferer or an AGC/ATTN ahead of the LNA.
2.3.13📖 Taylor model

Use $\cos^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1+\cos 2\omega t)$ and $\cos^3 = \tfrac{3}{4}\cos\omega t + \tfrac{1}{4}\cos 3\omega t$. With $V_0 = 0.4$ V:

(a) DC: $\tfrac{1}{2}a_2 V_0^2 = 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.16 = 0.024$ V.

(b) Fundamental amplitude: $a_1 V_0 + \tfrac{3}{4}a_3 V_0^3 = 5 \times 0.4 + 0.75 \times (-0.05) \times 0.064 = 2.0 - 0.0024 = 1.998$ V.

(c) 2nd harmonic: $\tfrac{1}{2}a_2 V_0^2 = 0.024$ V at $2\omega_0$.

(d) 3rd harmonic: $\tfrac{1}{4}a_3 V_0^3 = 0.25 \times (-0.05) \times 0.064 = -8\!\times\!10^{-4}$ V (i.e. $0.8$ mV at $3\omega_0$).

(e) Effective gain at $\omega_0$: $G_v = a_1 + \tfrac{3}{4}a_3 V_0^2 = 5 - 0.006 = 4.994$ ≈ 14 dB. Compression $\approx 0.01$ dB.

At 0.4 V drive the cubic correction is barely visible; the device is still in its linear region.
2.3.14📖 P1dB

(a) Linear region: $P_{out} = P_{in} + G = P_{in} + 18$. As $P_{in}$ rises, gain compresses; at $P_{1dB,out} = 10$ dBm, gain has dropped to 17 dB.

(b) $P_{1dB,in} = P_{1dB,out} - (G - 1) = 10 - 17 = -7$ dBm. At $P_{in} = -8$ dBm, the device is just below 1 dB compression — actual $G \approx 17.6$ dB → $P_{out} \approx -8 + 17.6 = 9.6$ dBm.

(c) $P_{1dB,in} = -7$ dBm.

Near $P_{1dB}$ each extra dB of input gives less than a dB of output — the curve flattens toward saturation.
2.3.15📖 IM products

$f_1 = 2400$, $f_2 = 2410$ MHz. Passband $2355\text{–}2455$ MHz.

IM2: $f_2 - f_1 = 10$ MHz (well below passband); $f_1 + f_2 = 4810$ MHz (well above); $2 f_1 = 4800$, $2 f_2 = 4820$ MHz (above). None fall in band.

IM3: $2f_1 - f_2 = 2390$ MHz, $2f_2 - f_1 = 2420$ MHz — both in-band (next to fundamentals). $3f_1 = 7200$ MHz, $3f_2 = 7230$ MHz, $2f_1 + f_2 = 7210$ MHz, $f_1 + 2f_2 = 7220$ MHz — far above passband.

The two in-band products $2f_1 - f_2$ and $2f_2 - f_1$ both come from the cubic $a_3$ term.

As always with closely-spaced two-tone IM, only the IM3 difference products land in band — and you can't filter them out without killing the wanted signals.
2.3.16📖 IP3

Slope-1 fundamental, slope-3 IM3 lines on a log-log $P_{out}$ vs $P_{in}$ chart.

Gap at $P_{in} = -10$ dBm: $\Delta = P_{out,fund} - P_{IM3} = 0 - (-50) = 50$ dB.

$\text{IIP}_3 = P_{in} + \Delta/2 = -10 + 25 = 15$ dBm.

$\text{OIP}_3 = \text{IIP}_3 + G = 15 + 10 = 25$ dBm.

IM3 ratio = 50 dBc — adequate for moderate-linearity systems but marginal for cellular base stations (which require > 70 dBc typically).

OIP3 is the imaginary intercept where the slope-1 and slope-3 lines cross — never reached in practice (P1dB hits first, $\sim 10$ dB below).
2.3.17📖 P1dB ↔ IP3 rule

(a) $OP_{1dB} \approx \text{OIP}_3 - 10.6 = 28 - 10.6 = 17.4$ dBm.

(b) $IP_{1dB,in} = OP_{1dB} - G_{1dB} \approx OP_{1dB} - (G - 1) = 17.4 - 24 = -6.6$ dBm.

(c) $\text{IIP}_3 = \text{OIP}_3 - G = 28 - 25 = 3$ dBm.

Limitation: the 10.6 dB constant comes from the strict cubic Taylor model. Real devices with significant 5th-order content, AM-PM distortion, or memory effects can deviate by $\pm 2$ dB or more — always verify with two-tone measurement when accuracy matters.

2.3.18📖 dynamic range

(a) $N_o = -174 + 10\log B + F + G = -174 + 60 + 4 + 30 = -80$ dBm.

(b) $DR_l = OP_{1dB} - N_o = 10 - (-80) = 90$ dB.

(c) $DR_f = (2/3)(\text{OIP}_3 - N_o) = (2/3)(20 - (-80)) = (2/3)(100) = 66.7$ dB.

(d) $DR_l - DR_f = 90 - 66.7 = 23.3$ dB ≈ 21 dB ✓ (the $\sim 21.3$ dB rule).

$DR_l$ uses the easy P1dB ceiling; $DR_f$ uses the harsher IM3-equals-noise ceiling — for in-band interference-limited systems, $DR_f$ is what matters.
2.3.19📖 cascaded IP3

Convert: $G_1 = 100$, $L = 4$ (so $G_2 = 0.25$), OIP3$_1 = 31.6$ mW, OIP3$_2 = 100$ mW.

$1/\text{OIP}_3 = 1/(G_2 \cdot \text{OIP}_3^{(1)}) + 1/\text{OIP}_3^{(2)} = 1/(0.25\times 31.6) + 1/100 = 1/7.9 + 1/100 = 0.1266 + 0.01 = 0.1366$.

OIP3$_{cas} = 7.32$ mW $= 8.65$ dBm.

Mixer dominates linearity ($1/(G_2\cdot \text{OIP}_3^{(1)}) = 1/7.9$ is 12.7× larger than $1/\text{OIP}_3^{(2)}$).

In cascaded IP3 the last stage dominates (because the signal level is highest there), opposite of noise figure where the first stage dominates. This is the fundamental design tension: low NF wants the LNA first; high IP3 wants the strongest device last.
2.3.20📖 PIM

(a) PIM3 / single carrier: $-125 - 43 = -168$ dBc — a typical good base-station spec ($< -150$ dBc).

(b) PIM3 vs noise floor: $-125 - (-110) = -15$ dB → PIM3 is $15$ dB below the receiver noise floor. Just below the noise — would corrupt sensitivity if it rose any further.

(c) PIM is generated by passive nonlinearities (bad connectors, oxidation, ferromagnetic parts). It only matters when powers are large — i.e. on the transmit side. The receiver's small signal levels can't generate appreciable PIM, so receivers don't worry about it. But the same antenna often sees both Tx and Rx signals (FDD), so PIM generated by the Tx can fall into the Rx band.

Cellular base-station antennas/duplexers must hold PIM3 $< -150$ dBc relative to a single carrier to keep the PIM landing in the Rx band below sensitivity. Outdoor exposure and connector aging make this a maintenance issue.

Quick Reference Summary

Important Symbols

Part 1 — Random Processes, PSD & Modulation

Symbol Name Units Notes
$S(f)$, $S(\omega)$Power spectral density (PSD)W/HzTwo-sided. Total power $P = \int S(f)\,df$
$R(\tau)$Autocorrelation functionW (or V²)$R(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S(f)\}$. $R(0)$ = total power
$n_0$Noise spectral density (one-sided)W/Hz$n_0 = kT$. Two-sided is $n_0/2$
$\delta(\tau)$Dirac delta function1/sWhite noise: $R(\tau) = (n_0/2)\delta(\tau)$ (uncorrelated)
$\sigma$, $\sigma^2$Noise standard deviation / varianceV / V²Gaussian PDF: $p(v) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}e^{-v^2/2\sigma^2}$
$V$ or $v_0$Peak signal voltageVPSK signal $s(t)=\pm V\cos\omega_c t$
$T$Bit/symbol periods$T = 1/R_b$ for binary signaling
$E_b$Energy per bitJ$E_b = V^2 T/2$ for sinusoidal PSK
$P_e$Bit error probability (BER)unitlessTypical target $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-9}$
$\mathrm{erfc}(x)$Complementary error functionunitless$\mathrm{erfc}(x) = 1 - \mathrm{erf}(x)$, Gaussian-tail probability
$\omega_c$, $f_c$Carrier (angular) frequencyrad/s, Hz$\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$
$M$Modulation orderintegerM-QAM, M-PSK; bits/symbol $= \log_2 M$

Part 2 — Noise Figure, Antenna & Distortion

Symbol Name Units Notes
$k$Boltzmann constant$1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K$10\log(k) = -228.6$ dBW/Hz/K
$T_0$Reference temperature$290$ KUsed in noise-figure definition; $kT_0 = -174$ dBm/Hz
$B$Noise bandwidthHzIF/channel bandwidth that defines noise power
$T_e$Equivalent noise temperatureKOf receiver/component; $T_e = (F-1)T_0$
$F$Noise figure (factor)linear / dB$F = \text{SNR}_{in}/\text{SNR}_{out}$ at $T_0=290$ K
$G$Available power gainlinear / dBAntenna gain in linear or dBi
$L$Loss factor$\ge 1$ / dBPassive lossy line at $T_p$: $T_e=(L-1)T_p$, $F=L$ when $T_p=T_0$
$T_b$Brightness (background) temperatureKBeam-weighted: cold sky 3–50 K, ground ~290 K
$T_p$Physical temperatureKOf antenna structure / lossy line
$T_a$ / $T_A$Antenna noise temperatureK$T_a = \eta_{rad}T_b + (1-\eta_{rad})T_p$
$\eta_{rad}$Radiation efficiency$0$–$1$Fraction radiated; $1-\eta_{rad}$ dissipated
$T_{SYS}$System noise temperatureK$T_{SYS} = T_a + T_e$ (referred to antenna)
EIRPEquivalent Isotropic Radiated PowerW / dBW$\text{EIRP} = G_t P_t$
FSPLFree-space path lossdB$20\log_{10}(4\pi R/\lambda)$
$G/T$Antenna figure of meritdB/K$G_r(\text{dBi}) - 10\log_{10}(T_a + T_e)$
$E_b/n_0$Energy-per-bit / noise PSDdBSets BER independent of modulation
$R_b$Bit ratebps$\text{CNR} = (E_b/n_0)(R_b/B)$
P1dB1 dB compression pointdBmInput or output (IP1dB / OP1dB)
IP3 / OIP33rd-order intercept pointdBmInput/output; OIP3 ≈ OP1dB + 10.6 dB
$DR_l$, $DR_f$Linear DR / Spurious-free DRdB$DR_l - DR_f \approx 21.3$ dB

Important Formulas

Part 1 — Random Processes, PSD & Modulation

Power ↔ PSD

$P = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(f)\,df$

Time avg = area under PSD

Wiener–Khinchin

$S(f) \;\longleftrightarrow\; R(\tau)$ (Fourier pair)

$R(0)$ = total power

White Noise PSD

$S(f) = n_0/2$ (two-sided)

$R(\tau) = (n_0/2)\,\delta(\tau)$

$n_0 = kT$

Band-Limited Noise

$P_n = n_0 B = kTB$

Integrated over physical $B$

Gaussian PDF

$p(v) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}\,e^{-v^2/2\sigma^2}$

Tail: $P(v>V)=\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{erfc}(V/\sqrt{2}\sigma)$

Energy per Bit

$E_b = \dfrac{V^2 T}{2}$ (PSK, sinusoidal)

$E_b = P_s \cdot T_b$

Integrate-and-Dump

Output signal: $VT$

$\sigma_{out}^2 = n_0/T$

$\Rightarrow E_b/n_0$ ratio

Universal BER (PSK)

$P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/n_0}\right)$

$10^{-5}$ → $E_b/n_0 = 9.6$ dB

Spectral Efficiency

$\eta_s = R_b/B$ (bps/Hz)

BPSK: 1, QPSK: 2, 16-QAM: 4, 64-QAM: 6

SNR ↔ $E_b/n_0$ (Part 1)

$\dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{E_b}{n_0}\cdot\dfrac{R_b}{B}$

Bridges symbol-rate and bandwidth views

Modulation Thresholds (BER $10^{-5}$)

Coherent PSK: 9.6 dB

Coherent FSK: 12.6 dB; Noncoh: 13.4 dB

Coherent ASK: 15.6 dB; Noncoh: 16.4 dB

QAM thresholds (BER $10^{-5}$)

16-QAM: $E_b/n_0 \approx 14.4$ dB

64-QAM: $E_b/n_0 \approx 22.5$ dB

SSB vs DSB-SC

SSB: SNR unchanged after demod

DSB-SC (coherent): +3 dB SNR gain

AM (DSB-LC) Efficiency

$\eta = \dfrac{m^2}{2+m^2}$, $m \le 1$

Max 33% at $m=1$ (carrier wastes power)

Part 2 — Noise Figure, Antenna & Distortion

Thermal Noise

$P_n = kTB$

$v_n = \sqrt{4kTBR}$

$kT_0 = -174$ dBm/Hz

Noise Figure ↔ $T_e$

$F = 1 + T_e/T_0$

$T_e = (F-1)T_0$

$T_0 = 290$ K

Friis Cascade ($F$)

$F = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2-1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3-1}{G_1G_2} + \cdots$

1st stage dominates → low-NF, high-gain LNA first

Cascade ($T_e$)

$T_e = T_1 + \dfrac{T_2}{G_1} + \dfrac{T_3}{G_1G_2} + \cdots$

Lossy Line

$T_e = (L-1)T_p$

$F = L$ when $T_p = T_0$

$F$(dB) $=$ IL(dB)

Antenna Noise Temp.

$T_a = \eta_{rad}T_b + (1-\eta_{rad})T_p$

Lossless: $T_a = T_b$

System $T_{SYS}$ (with line)

$T_{SYS} = T_a + T_{TL} + L_T\,T_{REC}$

$T_{TL} = (L_T-1)T_p$

Friis Transmission

$P_r = P_t G_t G_r\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{\!2}$

EIRP $= G_t P_t$

Free-Space Path Loss

FSPL $= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$

$= 32.4 + 20\log f_{\text{MHz}} + 20\log R_{\text{km}}$

G/T Figure of Merit

$G/T$ (dB/K) $= G_r$(dBi) $- 10\log_{10}(T_a + T_e)$

CNR (Link Budget)

CNR $= \text{EIRP} + G/T - \text{FSPL} - 10\log(kB)$

$10\log(kB) = -228.6 + 10\log B$

Min. Detectable Signal

$S_{i,min} = kB[T_a + (F-1)T_0]\,(S_o/N_o)_{min}$

SNR ↔ $E_b/n_0$

$S/N = (E_b/n_0)(R_b/B)$

BPSK/QPSK: $E_b/n_0 = 9.6$ dB → BER $10^{-5}$

BER (BPSK/QPSK)

$P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/n_0}\right)$

IP3 vs P1dB

OIP3 $\approx$ OP1dB $+ 10.6$ dB

IM3 slope = 3:1, fundamental = 1:1

Cascaded IP3 (output)

$\dfrac{1}{\text{OIP}_3} = \dfrac{1}{G_2\,\text{OIP}_{3,1}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{OIP}_{3,2}} + \cdots$

Last stage dominates (opposite of noise)

Dynamic Range

$DR_l = $ P1dB $- N_o$

$DR_f = \tfrac{2}{3}(\text{OIP}_3 - N_o)$

$DR_l - DR_f \approx 21.3$ dB

Y-factor (Noise Measurement)

$T_e = \dfrac{T_h - YT_c}{Y - 1}$

$Y = N_h/N_c$

Self-Study Checklist (0 / 0 done)

Part 1 — Random Processes, PSD & Modulation

Part 2 — Noise Figure, Antenna & Distortion

Reference: D.M. Pozar, Microwave Engineering, 4th Ed., §10(1–4), §14(1,2)  |  22 Practice Examples + 3 Homework Solutions
